title: Tank

infobox.value: is an [armoured fighting vehicle](/wiki/Armoured_fighting_vehicle "Armoured fighting vehicle") intended as a primary offensive weapon in [front-line](/wiki/Front_line "Front line") [ground combat](/wiki/Land_warfare "Land warfare"). Tank designs are a balance of heavy [firepower](/wiki/Firepower "Firepower"), strong [armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour"), and [battlefield](/wiki/Battlefield "Battlefield") [mobility](/wiki/Mobility_(military) "Mobility (military)") provided by [tracks](/wiki/Continuous_track "Continuous track") and a powerful engine; their main armament is often mounted within a [turret](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret"). They are a mainstay of modern 20th and 21st century ground forces and a key part of [combined arms](/wiki/Combined_arms "Combined arms") combat.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Tracked heavy armoured fighting vehicle

For other uses, see [Tank (disambiguation)](/wiki/Tank_(disambiguation) "Tank (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Mark_I_series_tank.jpg/500px-Mark_I_series_tank.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mark_I_series_tank.jpg)

The first tank to engage in battle, the British [Mark I tank](/wiki/Mark_I_tank "Mark I tank") (pictured in 1916) with the [Solomon](/wiki/Solomon_Joseph_Solomon "Solomon Joseph Solomon") camouflage scheme

A **tank** is an [armoured fighting vehicle](/wiki/Armoured_fighting_vehicle "Armoured fighting vehicle") intended as a primary offensive weapon in [front-line](/wiki/Front_line "Front line") [ground combat](/wiki/Land_warfare "Land warfare"). Tank designs are a balance of heavy [firepower](/wiki/Firepower "Firepower"), strong [armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour"), and [battlefield](/wiki/Battlefield "Battlefield") [mobility](/wiki/Mobility_(military) "Mobility (military)") provided by [tracks](/wiki/Continuous_track "Continuous track") and a powerful engine; their main armament is often mounted within a [turret](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret"). They are a mainstay of modern 20th and 21st century ground forces and a key part of [combined arms](/wiki/Combined_arms "Combined arms") combat.

[![German tank Pz.Kpfw. VI Ausf. B "Tiger II" (223) of the 503rd heavy tank battalion on the streets of Budapest October 1944.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/German_tank_Pz.Kpfw._VI_Ausf._B_%22Tiger_II%22_%28223%29_of_the_503rd_heavy_tank_battalion_on_the_streets_of_Budapest_October_1944._%2850023567543%29.jpg/250px-German_tank_Pz.Kpfw._VI_Ausf._B_%22Tiger_II%22_%28223%29_of_the_503rd_heavy_tank_battalion_on_the_streets_of_Budapest_October_1944._%2850023567543%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:German_tank_Pz.Kpfw._VI_Ausf._B_%22Tiger_II%22_(223)_of_the_503rd_heavy_tank_battalion_on_the_streets_of_Budapest_October_1944._(50023567543).jpg)

German tank [Pz.Kpfw. VI Ausf. B "Tiger II" (223)](/wiki/Tiger_II "Tiger II")

[![Leopard 2A8 tank (KMW) representing newest generation of Main Battle Tanks equipped with anti drone systems.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Tank_Leopard_2A7_NATO_Days_2022_%28no_marks%29.png/250px-Tank_Leopard_2A7_NATO_Days_2022_%28no_marks%29.png)](/wiki/File:Tank_Leopard_2A7_NATO_Days_2022_(no_marks).png)

[Leopard 2A8](/wiki/Leopard_2 "Leopard 2") tank (KMW)

Modern tanks are versatile mobile land [weapons platforms](/wiki/Weapons_platform "Weapons platform") whose main armament is a large-[calibre](/wiki/Calibre "Calibre") [tank gun](/wiki/Tank_gun "Tank gun") mounted in a rotating [gun turret](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret"), supplemented by [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun") or other ranged weapons such as [anti-tank guided missiles](/wiki/Anti-tank_guided_missile "Anti-tank guided missile") or [rocket launchers](/wiki/Rocket_launcher "Rocket launcher"). They have heavy [vehicle armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour") which provides protection for the crew, the vehicle's munition storage, [fuel tank](/wiki/Fuel_tank "Fuel tank") and propulsion systems. The use of tracks rather than wheels provides improved [operational mobility](/wiki/Operational_mobility "Operational mobility") which allows the tank to overcome rugged terrain and adverse conditions such as mud and ice/snow better than wheeled vehicles, and thus be more flexibly positioned at advantageous locations on the battlefield. These features enable the tank to perform in a variety of intense combat situations, simultaneously both [offensively](/wiki/Offensive_(military) "Offensive (military)") (with [direct fire](/wiki/Direct_fire "Direct fire") from their powerful main gun) and [defensively](/wiki/Defence_(military) "Defence (military)") (as [fire support](/wiki/Fire_support "Fire support") and [defilade](/wiki/Defilade "Defilade") for friendly troops due to the near invulnerability to common infantry [small arms](/wiki/Small_arm "Small arm") and good resistance against heavier weapons, although anti-tank weapons used in 2022, some of them man-portable, have demonstrated the ability to destroy older generations of tanks with single shots[[1]](#cite_note-6000nlaw-1)),[*[disputed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Disputed statement") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Tank#Near_invulnerability_to_small_arms_and_good_resistance_against_heavier_weapons "Talk:Tank")*] all while maintaining the mobility needed to exploit changing tactical situations.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Fully integrating tanks into modern military forces spawned a new era of combat called [armoured warfare](/wiki/Armoured_warfare "Armoured warfare").

Until the invention of the [main battle tank](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank"), tanks were typically categorized either by [weight class](/wiki/Weight_class "Weight class") ([ultralight](/wiki/Tankette "Tankette"), [light](/wiki/Light_tank "Light tank"), [medium](/wiki/Medium_tank "Medium tank"), [heavy](/wiki/Heavy_tank "Heavy tank") or [superheavy tanks](/wiki/Superheavy_tank "Superheavy tank")) or doctrinal purpose ([breakthrough-](/wiki/Breakthrough_(military) "Breakthrough (military)"), [cavalry-](/wiki/Cavalry_tank "Cavalry tank"), [infantry-](/wiki/Infantry_tank "Infantry tank"), [cruiser-](/wiki/Cruiser_tank "Cruiser tank"), antinfantry-, [antitank-](/wiki/Anti-tank_warfare "Anti-tank warfare"), operational-, qualitative reinforcement-, [combined arms](/wiki/Combined_arms "Combined arms")-, [special operations](/wiki/Special_operations "Special operations")-, or [reconnaissance](/wiki/Armoured_reconnaissance "Armoured reconnaissance") tanks). Some are larger and more thickly armoured and with large guns, while others are smaller, lightly armoured, and equipped with a smaller caliber and lighter gun. These smaller tanks move over terrain with speed and agility and can perform a reconnaissance role in addition to engaging hostile targets. The smaller, faster tank would not normally engage in battle with a larger, heavily armoured tank, except during a surprise [flanking manoeuvre](/wiki/Flanking_manoeuvre "Flanking manoeuvre").

## Etymology

The word *tank* was first applied in a military context to British "landships" in 1915 to keep their nature secret before they entered service.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Origins

On 24 December 1915, a meeting took place at the Inter-Departmental Conference (including representatives of the Director of Naval Construction's Committee, the Admiralty, the Ministry of Munitions, and the War Office). Its purpose was to discuss the progress of the plans for what were described as "Caterpillar Machine Gun Destroyers or Land Cruisers." In his autobiography, [Albert Gerald Stern](/wiki/Albert_Gerald_Stern "Albert Gerald Stern") (Secretary to the Landship Committee, later head of the Mechanical Warfare Supply Department) says that at that meeting:

> [Mr. (Thomas J.) Macnamara](/wiki/Thomas_James_Macnamara "Thomas James Macnamara") ([M.P.](/wiki/Member_of_parliament "Member of parliament"), and [Parliamentary and Financial Secretary to the Admiralty](/wiki/Secretary_to_the_Admiralty "Secretary to the Admiralty")) then suggested, for secrecy's sake, to change the title of the Landship Committee. [Mr. d'Eyncourt](/wiki/Sir_Eustace_Tennyson-d%27Eyncourt,_1st_Baronet "Sir Eustace Tennyson-d'Eyncourt, 1st Baronet") agreed that it was very desirable to retain secrecy by all means, and proposed to refer to the vessel as a "Water Carrier". In Government offices, committees and departments are always known by their initials. For this reason I, as Secretary, considered the proposed title totally unsuitable.[[a]](#cite_note-5) In our search for a synonymous term, we changed the word "Water Carrier" to "Tank," and became the "Tank Supply" or "T.S." Committee. That is how these weapons came to be called Tanks.

He incorrectly added, "and the name has now been adopted by all countries in the world."[[5]](#cite_note-6)

Lieutenant-Colonel Ernest Swinton, who was secretary to the meeting, says that he was instructed to find a non-committal word when writing his report of the proceedings. In the evening he discussed it with a fellow officer, [Lt-Col Walter Dally Jones](/wiki/Walter_Dally_Jones "Walter Dally Jones"), and they chose the word "tank". "That night, in the draft report of the conference, the word 'tank' was employed in its new sense for the first time."[[6]](#cite_note-7) Swinton's *Notes on the Employment of Tanks*, in which he uses the word throughout, was published in January 1916.

In July 1918, *Popular Science Monthly* reported:

> Because a fellow of the [Royal Historical Society](/wiki/Royal_Historical_Society "Royal Historical Society")\* has unintentionally misled the British public as to the origin of the famous "tanks", [Sir William Tritton](/wiki/Sir_William_Tritton "Sir William Tritton"), who designed and built them, has published the real story of their name ... Since it was obviously inadvisable to herald "Little Willie's" reason for existence to the world he was known as the "Instructional Demonstration Unit." "Little Willie's" hull was called in the shop orders a "water carrier for Mesopotamia"; no one knew that the hull was intended to be mounted on a truck. Naturally, the water carrier began to be called a "tank". So the name came to be used by managers and foremen of the shop, until now it has a place in the army vocabulary and will probably be so known in history for all time.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

(\*F.J. Gardiner, F.R.Hist.S.)

D'Eyncourt's account differs from Swinton's and Tritton's:

> ... when the future arrangements were under discussion for transporting the first landships to France a question arose as to how, from a security point of view, the consignment should be labelled. To justify their size we decided to call them 'water-carriers for Russia' —the idea being that they should be taken for some new method of taking water to forward troops in the battle areas. Lt.-Col. Swinton ... raised a humorous objection to this, remarking that the War Office pundits would probably contract the description to 'W.C.'s for Russia', and that we had better forestall this by merely labelling the packages 'Tanks'. So tanks they became, and tanks they have remained."[[8]](#cite_note-9)

This appears to be an imperfect recollection. He says that the name problem arose "when we shipped the first two vehicles to France the following year" (August 1916), but by that time the name "tank" had been in use for eight months. The tanks were labelled "With Care to Petrograd," but the belief was encouraged that they were a type of snowplough.

The term "tank" is used throughout the English-speaking world, but other countries use different terminology. In France, the second country to use tanks in battle, the word *tank* or *tanque* was adopted initially, but was then, largely at the insistence of [Colonel J.B.E. Estienne](/wiki/Jean_Baptiste_Eug%C3%A8ne_Estienne "Jean Baptiste Eugène Estienne"), rejected in favour of *char d'assaut* ("assault vehicle") or simply *char* ("vehicle"). During World War I, German sources tended to refer to British tanks as *tanks*[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) and to their own as *Kampfwagen*.[[11]](#cite_note-12) Later, tanks became referred to as "*Panzer*" (lit. 'armour'), a shortened form of the full term "*Panzerkampfwagen*", literally "armoured fighting vehicle". In [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), tanks are called *Dabbāba*.[[12]](#cite_note-13) The same word is used in [Turoyo](/wiki/Turoyo "Turoyo") (a western dialect of [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic")), but [Swadaya](/wiki/Swadaya "Swadaya"), an eastern dialect, uses *rashupta* instead.[[13]](#cite_note-14) In Italian, a tank is a "*carro armato*" (lit. 'armed wagon').[[14]](#cite_note-15) Norway uses the term *stridsvogn*[[15]](#cite_note-16) and Sweden the similar *stridsvagn* (lit. 'battle wagon', also used for chariots),[[16]](#cite_note-17) whereas Denmark uses *kampvogn* (lit. 'fight wagon').[[17]](#cite_note-18) Finland uses *panssarivaunu* (armoured wagon),[[18]](#cite_note-19) although *tankki* is also used colloquially.[[19]](#cite_note-20) The Polish name *czołg*, derived from verb *czołgać się* ("to crawl"), is used, depicting the vehicle's movement and its speed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In Hungarian, the tank is called *harckocsi* (combat wagon), albeit *tank* is also common.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In Japanese, the term *sensha* (戦車, lit. "battle vehicle") is taken from Chinese and used, and this term is likewise borrowed into Korean as *jeoncha* (전차/戰車); more recent Chinese literature uses the English-derived 坦克 *tǎnkè* (tank) as opposed to 戰車 *zhànchē* (battle vehicle) used in earlier days.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Development overview

The modern tank is the result of a century of development from the first primitive armoured vehicles, due to improvements in technology such as the internal combustion engine, which allowed the rapid movement of heavy armoured vehicles. As a result of these advances, tanks underwent tremendous shifts in capability in the years since their first appearance. [Tanks in World War I](/wiki/Tanks_in_World_War_I "Tanks in World War I") were developed separately and simultaneously by Great Britain and France as a means to break the deadlock of [trench warfare](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare") on the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(WWI) "Western Front (WWI)"). The first British prototype, nicknamed [Little Willie](/wiki/Little_Willie "Little Willie"), was constructed at [William Foster & Co.](/wiki/William_Foster_%26_Co. "William Foster & Co.") in [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln,_England "Lincoln, England"), England in 1915, with leading roles played by Major [Walter Gordon Wilson](/wiki/Walter_Gordon_Wilson "Walter Gordon Wilson") who designed the gearbox and hull, and by [William Tritton](/wiki/William_Tritton "William Tritton") of [William Foster and Co.](/wiki/William_Foster_%26_Co. "William Foster & Co."), who designed the track plates.[[20]](#cite_note-Tank_origins-21) This was a prototype of a new design that would become the British Army's [Mark I tank](/wiki/Mark_I_tank "Mark I tank"), the first tank used in combat in September 1916 during the [Battle of the Somme](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Somme "Battle of the Somme").[[20]](#cite_note-Tank_origins-21) The name "tank" was adopted by the British during the early stages of their development, as a security measure to conceal their purpose (see [etymology](#Etymology)). While the British and French built thousands of tanks in World War I, Germany was unconvinced of the tank's potential, and did not have enough resources, thus it built only twenty.

[Tanks of the interwar period](/wiki/Tanks_of_the_interwar_period "Tanks of the interwar period") evolved into the much larger and more powerful [designs of World War II](/wiki/Tanks_in_World_War_II "Tanks in World War II"). Important new concepts of armoured warfare were developed; the Soviet Union launched [the first mass tank/air attack at Khalkhin Gol](/wiki/Battles_of_Khalkhin_Gol "Battles of Khalkhin Gol") ([Nomonhan](/wiki/Nomonhan "Nomonhan")) in August 1939,[[21]](#cite_note-22) and later developed the [T-34](/wiki/T-34 "T-34"), one of the predecessors of the [main battle tank](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank"). Less than two weeks later, Germany began their large-scale armoured campaigns that would become known as [blitzkrieg](/wiki/Blitzkrieg "Blitzkrieg") ("lightning war") – massed concentrations of tanks combined with [motorized](/wiki/Motorized_infantry "Motorized infantry") and [mechanized infantry](/wiki/Mechanized_infantry "Mechanized infantry"), [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") and [air power](/wiki/Air_power "Air power") designed to break through the [enemy front](/wiki/Front_(military) "Front (military)") and collapse enemy resistance.

The widespread introduction of [high-explosive anti-tank](/wiki/High-explosive_anti-tank "High-explosive anti-tank") warheads during the second half of World War II led to lightweight infantry-carried anti-tank weapons such as the [Panzerfaust](/wiki/Panzerfaust "Panzerfaust"), which could destroy some types of tanks. [Tanks in the Cold War](/wiki/Tanks_in_the_Cold_War "Tanks in the Cold War") were designed with these weapons in mind, and led to greatly improved armour types during the 1960s, especially [composite armour](/wiki/Composite_armour "Composite armour"). Improved engines, transmissions and suspensions allowed tanks of this period to grow larger. Aspects of gun technology changed significantly as well, with advances in shell design and aiming technology.

During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), the [main battle tank](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank") concept arose and became a key component of modern armies.[[22]](#cite_note-House1984-23) In the 21st century, with the increasing role of [asymmetrical warfare](/wiki/Asymmetrical_warfare "Asymmetrical warfare") and the end of the Cold War, that also contributed to the increase of cost-effective [anti-tank rocket propelled grenades](/wiki/Rocket_propelled_grenade "Rocket propelled grenade") (RPGs) worldwide and its successors, the ability of tanks to operate independently has declined. Modern tanks are more frequently organized into [combined arms](/wiki/Combined_arms "Combined arms") units which involve the support of [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry"), who may accompany the tanks in [infantry fighting vehicles](/wiki/Infantry_fighting_vehicle "Infantry fighting vehicle"), and supported by [reconnaissance](/wiki/Reconnaissance_aircraft "Reconnaissance aircraft") or [ground-attack aircraft](/wiki/Ground-attack_aircraft "Ground-attack aircraft").[[23]](#cite_note-24)

Main article: [History of the tank](/wiki/History_of_the_tank "History of the tank")

### 20th-century

#### Conceptions

The tank is the 20th-century realization of an ancient concept: that of providing troops with mobile protection and firepower. The [internal combustion engine](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine"), [armour plate](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour"), and [continuous track](/wiki/Continuous_track "Continuous track") were key innovations leading to the invention of the modern tank.

During the 119 BC [Battle of Mobei](/wiki/Battle_of_Mobei "Battle of Mobei") of the [Han–Xiongnu War](/wiki/Han%E2%80%93Xiongnu_War "Han–Xiongnu War"), the [Han](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") general [Wei Qing](/wiki/Wei_Qing "Wei Qing") led his army through a fatiguing expeditionary march across the [Gobi desert](/wiki/Gobi_desert "Gobi desert") only to find [Yizhixie](/wiki/Yizhixie "Yizhixie") [chanyu](/wiki/Chanyu "Chanyu")'s main force waiting to encircle them on the other side. Using armored heavy wagons known as "Wu Gang Wagon" ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 武剛車) in ring formations that provided Chinese [archers](/wiki/Archery "Archery"), [crossbowmen](/wiki/Crossbowmen "Crossbowmen") and [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") protection from the Xiongnu's powerful [cavalry charges](/wiki/Charge_(warfare) "Charge (warfare)"), and allowed Han troops to utilize their ranged weapons' advantages of [precision](/wiki/Accuracy_and_precision "Accuracy and precision"). This forced a [stalemate](/wiki/Stalemate "Stalemate") and allowed time for his troops to recover strength, before using the cover of a [sandstorm](/wiki/Sandstorm "Sandstorm") to launch a counteroffensive which overran the [nomads](/wiki/Nomad "Nomad").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/DaVinciTankAtAmboise.jpeg/250px-DaVinciTankAtAmboise.jpeg)](/wiki/File:DaVinciTankAtAmboise.jpeg)

Model of [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci")'s [fighting vehicle](/wiki/Leonardo%27s_fighting_vehicle "Leonardo's fighting vehicle")

Many sources imply that [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci") and [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells") in some way foresaw or "invented" the tank. Leonardo's late-15th-century drawings of what some describe as a "tank" show a man-powered, wheeled vehicle surrounded by cannons. However, the human crew would have difficulty moving the heavy vehicle over long distances, while usage of animals was problematic in a space so confined. In the 15th century, [Jan Žižka](/wiki/Jan_%C5%BDi%C5%BEka "Jan Žižka") built armoured wagons known as ‘*Wagenburg*’ containing cannons and used them effectively in several battles during the [Hussite-wars](/wiki/Hussite_Wars "Hussite Wars"). The continuous "[caterpillar track](/wiki/Continuous_track "Continuous track")" arose from attempts to improve the mobility of wheeled vehicles by spreading their weight, reducing ground pressure, and increasing their traction. Experiments can be traced back as far as the 17th century, and by the late nineteenth they existed in various recognizable and practical forms in several countries.

It is frequently claimed that [Richard Lovell Edgeworth](/wiki/Richard_Lovell_Edgeworth "Richard Lovell Edgeworth") created a caterpillar track. It is true that in 1770 he patented a "machine, that should carry and lay down its own road", but this was Edgeworth's choice of words. His own account in his autobiography is of a horse-drawn wooden carriage on eight retractable legs, capable of lifting itself over high walls. The description bears no similarity to a caterpillar track.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Armoured trains appeared in the mid-19th century, and various armoured steam and petrol-engined vehicles were also proposed.

The machines described in Wells's 1903 short story *[The Land Ironclads](/wiki/The_Land_Ironclads "The Land Ironclads")* are a step closer, insofar as they are armour-plated, have an internal power plant, and are able to cross trenches.[[25]](#cite_note-26) Some aspects of the story foresee the tactical use and impact of the tanks that later came into being. However, Wells's vehicles were driven by steam and moved on [pedrail wheels](/wiki/Pedrail_wheel "Pedrail wheel"), technologies that were already outdated at the time of writing. After seeing British tanks in 1916, Wells denied having "invented" them, writing, "Yet let me state at once that I was not their prime originator. I took up an idea, manipulated it slightly, and handed it on."[[26]](#cite_note-27) It is, though, possible that one of the British tank pioneers, [Ernest Swinton](/wiki/Ernest_Swinton "Ernest Swinton"), was subconsciously or otherwise influenced by Wells's tale.[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)

The first combinations of the three principal components of the tank appeared in the decade before World War One. In 1903, Captain Léon René Levavasseur of the French artillery [proposed mounting a field gun in an armoured box on tracks](/wiki/Levavasseur_project "Levavasseur project"). Major William E. Donohue, of the British Army's Mechanical Transport Committee, suggested fixing a gun and armoured shield on a British type of track-driven vehicle.[[29]](#cite_note-30) The first [armoured car](/wiki/Armored_car_(military) "Armored car (military)") was produced in Austria in 1904. However, all were restricted to rails or reasonably passable terrain. It was the development of a practical caterpillar track that provided the necessary independent, all-terrain mobility.

In a memorandum of 1908, Antarctic explorer [Robert Falcon Scott](/wiki/Robert_Falcon_Scott "Robert Falcon Scott") presented his view that man-hauling to the South Pole was impossible and that motor traction was needed.[[30]](#cite_note-31) Snow vehicles did not yet exist, however, and so his engineer [Reginald Skelton](/wiki/Reginald_Skelton "Reginald Skelton") developed the idea of a caterpillar track for snow surfaces.[[31]](#cite_note-32) These tracked motors were built by the [Wolseley Tool and Motor Car Company](/wiki/Wolseley_Motors "Wolseley Motors") in Birmingham and tested in Switzerland and Norway, and can be seen in action in [Herbert Ponting](/wiki/Herbert_Ponting "Herbert Ponting")'s 1911 documentary film of Scott's Antarctic [Terra Nova Expedition](/wiki/Terra_Nova_Expedition "Terra Nova Expedition").[[32]](#cite_note-33) Scott died during the expedition in 1912, but expedition member and biographer [Apsley Cherry-Garrard](/wiki/Apsley_Cherry-Garrard "Apsley Cherry-Garrard") credited Scott's "motors" with the inspiration for the British World War I tanks, writing: "Scott never knew their true possibilities; for they were the direct ancestors of the 'tanks' in France".[[33]](#cite_note-34)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

In 1911, a Lieutenant Engineer in the Austrian Army, [Günther Burstyn](/wiki/G%C3%BCnther_Burstyn "Günther Burstyn"), presented to the Austrian and Prussian War Ministries plans for a light, three-man tank with a gun in a revolving turret, the so-called Burstyn-Motorgeschütz.[[34]](#cite_note-35) In the same year an Australian civil engineer named [Lancelot de Mole](/wiki/Lancelot_de_Mole "Lancelot de Mole") submitted a basic design for a tracked, armoured vehicle to the British [War Office](/wiki/War_Office "War Office").[[35]](#cite_note-36) In Russia, Vasiliy Mendeleev designed a tracked vehicle containing a large naval gun.[[36]](#cite_note-37) All of these ideas were rejected and, by 1914, forgotten (although it was officially acknowledged after the war that de Mole's design was at least the equal to the initial British tanks). Various individuals continued to contemplate the use of tracked vehicles for military applications, but by the outbreak of the War no one in a position of responsibility in any army seems to have given much thought to tanks.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### World War I

Film clip of World War I-era tanks

Main article: [Tanks in World War I](/wiki/Tanks_in_World_War_I "Tanks in World War I")

##### United Kingdom

> The direct military impact of the tank can be debated but its effect on the Germans was immense, it caused bewilderment, terror and concern in equal measure. It was also a huge boost to the civilians at home. After facing the Zeppelins, at last Britain had a wonder weapon. Tanks were taken on tours and treated almost like film stars.

— David Willey, curator at [The Tank Museum](/wiki/The_Tank_Museum "The Tank Museum"), Bovington.[[20]](#cite_note-Tank_origins-21)

From late 1914 a small number of middle-ranking [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") officers tried to persuade the War Office and the Government to consider the creation of armoured vehicles. Amongst their suggestions was the use of caterpillar tractors, but although the Army used many such vehicles for towing heavy guns, it could not be persuaded that they could be adapted as armoured vehicles. The consequence was that early tank development in the United Kingdom was carried out by the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/British_Mark_V-star_Tank.jpg/330px-British_Mark_V-star_Tank.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_Mark_V-star_Tank.jpg)

British World War I Mark V\* tank

As the result of an approach by Royal Naval Air Service officers who had been operating armoured cars on the Western Front, the [First Lord of the Admiralty](/wiki/First_Lord_of_the_Admiralty "First Lord of the Admiralty"), [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), formed the [Landship Committee](/wiki/Landship_Committee "Landship Committee"), on 20 February 1915.[[37]](#cite_note-Churchill-38) The [Director of Naval Construction](/wiki/Director_of_Naval_Construction "Director of Naval Construction") for the Royal Navy, [Eustace Tennyson d'Eyncourt](/wiki/Eustace_Tennyson_d%27Eyncourt "Eustace Tennyson d'Eyncourt"), was appointed to head the Committee in view of his experience with the engineering methods it was felt might be required; the two other members were naval officers, and a number of industrialists were engaged as consultants. So many played a part in its long and complicated development that it is not possible to name any individual as the sole inventor of the tank.[[37]](#cite_note-Churchill-38)

However leading roles were played by Lt [Walter Gordon Wilson](/wiki/Walter_Gordon_Wilson "Walter Gordon Wilson") R.N. who designed the gearbox and developed practical tracks and by [William Tritton](/wiki/William_Tritton "William Tritton") whose agricultural machinery company, [William Foster & Co.](/wiki/William_Foster_%26_Co. "William Foster & Co.") in [Lincoln, Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincoln,_Lincolnshire "Lincoln, Lincolnshire"), England built the [prototypes](/wiki/Prototype "Prototype").[[20]](#cite_note-Tank_origins-21)[[38]](#cite_note-39) On 22 July 1915, a commission was placed to design a machine that could cross a trench 4 ft wide.[[20]](#cite_note-Tank_origins-21) Secrecy surrounded the project with the designers locking themselves in a room at the White Hart Hotel in Lincoln.[[20]](#cite_note-Tank_origins-21) The committee's first design, [Little Willie](/wiki/Little_Willie "Little Willie"), ran for the first time in September 1915 and served to develop the form of the track but an improved design, better able to cross trenches, swiftly followed and in January 1916 the prototype, nicknamed "Mother", was adopted as the design for future tanks. The first order for tanks was placed on 12 February 1916, and a second on 21 April. Fosters built 37 (all "male"), and [Metropolitan Railway Carriage and Wagon Company](/wiki/Metro-Cammell "Metro-Cammell"), of Birmingham, 113 (38 "male" and 75 "female"), a total of 150.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Production models of ["Male"](/wiki/Male_tank "Male tank") tanks (armed with naval cannon and machine guns) and ["Females"](/wiki/Female_tank "Female tank") (carrying only machine-guns) would go on to fight in history's first tank action at the Somme in September 1916.[[37]](#cite_note-Churchill-38)[[40]](#cite_note-41) Great Britain produced about 2,600 tanks of various types during the war.[[41]](#cite_note-42) The first tank to engage in battle was designated *D1*, a British [Mark I](/wiki/Mark_I_tank "Mark I tank") Male, during the [Battle of Flers-Courcelette](/wiki/Battle_of_Flers-Courcelette "Battle of Flers-Courcelette") (part of the wider [Somme offensive](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Somme "Battle of the Somme")) on 15 September 1916.[[42]](#cite_note-43) Bert Chaney, a nineteen-year-old signaller with the 7th London Territorial Battalion, reported that "three huge mechanical monsters such as [he] had never seen before" rumbled their way onto the battlefield, "frightening the [Jerries](/wiki/List_of_terms_used_for_Germans#Jerry "List of terms used for Germans") out of their wits and making them scuttle like frightened rabbits."[[43]](#cite_note-44) When the news of the first use of the tanks emerged, Prime Minister [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George") commented,

> It is really to Mr Winston Churchill that the credit is due more than to anyone else. He took up with enthusiasm the idea of making them a long time ago, and he met with many difficulties. He converted me, and at the [Ministry of Munitions](/wiki/Ministry_of_Munitions "Ministry of Munitions") he went ahead and made them. The admiralty experts were invaluable, and gave the greatest possible assistance. They are, of course, experts in the matter of armour plating. [Major Stern](/wiki/Albert_Gerald_Stern "Albert Gerald Stern"), (formerly an officer in the Royal Naval Air Service) a business man at the Ministry of Munitions had charge of the work of getting them built, and he did the task very well. [Col Swinton](/wiki/Ernest_Swinton "Ernest Swinton") and others also did valuable work.

— David Lloyd George, 19 September 1916.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

##### France

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/American_troops_going_forward_to_the_battle_line_in_the_Forest_of_Argonne._France%2C_September_26%2C_1918._-_NARA_-_530748.jpg/250px-American_troops_going_forward_to_the_battle_line_in_the_Forest_of_Argonne._France%2C_September_26%2C_1918._-_NARA_-_530748.jpg)](/wiki/File:American_troops_going_forward_to_the_battle_line_in_the_Forest_of_Argonne._France,_September_26,_1918._-_NARA_-_530748.jpg)

French [Renault FT](/wiki/Renault_FT "Renault FT") tanks, here operated by the US army, pioneered the use of a fully traversable turret and served as pattern for most modern tanks.

Whilst several experimental machines were investigated in France, it was a colonel of artillery, [J.B.E. Estienne](/wiki/Jean_Baptiste_Eug%C3%A8ne_Estienne "Jean Baptiste Eugène Estienne"), who directly approached the Commander-in-Chief with detailed plans for a tank on caterpillar tracks, in late 1915. The result was two largely unsatisfactory types of tank, 400 each of the [Schneider](/wiki/Schneider_CA1 "Schneider CA1") and [Saint-Chamond](/wiki/Saint-Chamond_(tank) "Saint-Chamond (tank)"), both based on the [Holt tractor](/wiki/Holt_tractor "Holt tractor").

The following year, the French pioneered the use of a full 360° rotation [turret](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret") in a tank for the first time, with the creation of the [Renault FT](/wiki/Renault_FT "Renault FT") light tank, with the turret containing the tank's main armament. In addition to the traversable turret, another innovative feature of the FT was its engine located at the rear. This pattern, with the gun located in a mounted turret and the engine at the back, has become the standard for most succeeding tanks across the world even to this day.[[45]](#cite_note-46) The FT was the most numerous tank of the war; over 3,000 were made by late 1918.

##### Germany

Germany fielded very few tanks during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), and started development only after encountering British tanks on the Somme. The [A7V](/wiki/A7V "A7V"), the only type made, was introduced in March 1918 with just 20 being produced during the war.[[46]](#cite_note-47) The first tank *versus* tank action took place on 24 April 1918 at the [Second Battle of Villers-Bretonneux](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Villers-Bretonneux "Second Battle of Villers-Bretonneux"), France, when three British [Mark IVs](/wiki/Mark_IV_tank "Mark IV tank") met three German [A7Vs](/wiki/A7V "A7V"). Captured British Mk IVs formed the bulk of Germany's tank forces during World War I; about 35 were in service at any one time. Plans to expand the tank programme were under way when the War ended.

##### United States

The United States [Tank Corps](/wiki/Tank_Corps_of_the_American_Expeditionary_Force "Tank Corps of the American Expeditionary Force") used tanks supplied by France and Great Britain during World War I. Production of American-built tanks had just begun when the War came to an end.

##### Italy

Italy also manufactured two [Fiat 2000s](/wiki/Fiat_2000 "Fiat 2000") towards the end of the war, too late to see service.

##### Russia

Russia independently built and trialed two prototypes early in the War; the tracked, two-man [Vezdekhod](/wiki/Vezdekhod "Vezdekhod") and the huge [Lebedenko](/wiki/Tsar_Tank "Tsar Tank"), but neither went into production. A tracked self-propelled gun was also designed but not produced.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

Although tank tactics developed rapidly during the war, piecemeal deployments, mechanical problems, and poor mobility limited the military significance of the tank in World War I, and the tank did not fulfil its promise of rendering trench warfare obsolete. Nonetheless, it was clear to military thinkers on both sides that tanks in some way could have a significant role in future conflicts.[[48]](#cite_note-Willmott2003-49)

#### Interwar period

Main article: [Tanks of the interwar period](/wiki/Tanks_of_the_interwar_period "Tanks of the interwar period")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Hotchkiss_H-39.jpg/250px-Hotchkiss_H-39.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hotchkiss_H-39.jpg)

French [Hotchkiss H-39 light tank of 1939](/wiki/Hotchkiss_H35#The_Char_léger_modèle_1935_H_modifié_39 "Hotchkiss H35")

In the [interwar period](/wiki/History_of_the_tank#Between_the_wars "History of the tank") tanks underwent further mechanical development. In terms of tactics, [J.F.C. Fuller](/wiki/J.F.C._Fuller "J.F.C. Fuller")'s doctrine of spearhead attacks with massed tank formations was the basis for work by [Heinz Guderian](/wiki/Heinz_Guderian "Heinz Guderian") in Germany, [Percy Hobart](/wiki/Percy_Hobart "Percy Hobart") in Britain, [Adna R. Chaffee, Jr.](/wiki/Adna_R._Chaffee,_Jr. "Adna R. Chaffee, Jr."), in the US, [Charles de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle") in France, and [Mikhail Tukhachevsky](/wiki/Mikhail_Tukhachevsky "Mikhail Tukhachevsky") in the USSR. [Liddell Hart](/wiki/B._H._Liddell_Hart "B. H. Liddell Hart") held a more moderate view that all arms – cavalry, infantry and artillery – should be mechanized and work together. The British formed the all-arms [Experimental Mechanized Force](/wiki/Experimental_Mechanized_Force "Experimental Mechanized Force") to test the use of tanks with supporting forces.

In the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") only Germany would initially put the theory into practice on a large scale, and it was their superior tactics and French blunders, not superior weapons, that made the "blitzkrieg" so successful in May 1940.[[49]](#cite_note-Deighton1979-50) For information regarding tank development in this period, see [tank development between the wars](/wiki/History_of_the_tank#Between_the_wars "History of the tank").

Germany, Italy and the Soviet Union all experimented heavily with tank warfare during their clandestine and "volunteer" involvement in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"), which saw some of the earliest examples of successful mechanized combined arms —such as when [Republican](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") troops, equipped with Soviet-supplied tanks and supported by aircraft, eventually routed Italian troops fighting for the [Nationalists](/wiki/Nationalists "Nationalists") in the seven-day [Battle of Guadalajara](/wiki/Battle_of_Guadalajara "Battle of Guadalajara") in 1937.[[50]](#cite_note-51) However, of the nearly 700 tanks deployed during this conflict, only about 64 tanks representing the *Franco* faction and 331 from the *Republican* side were equipped with cannon, and of those 64 nearly all were [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") vintage [Renault FT](/wiki/Renault_FT "Renault FT") tanks, while the 331 Soviet supplied machines had 45mm main guns and were of 1930s manufacture.[[51]](#cite_note-52) The balance of *Nationalist* tanks were machine gun armed. The primary lesson learned from this war was that machine gun armed tanks had to be equipped with cannon, with the associated armour inherent to modern tanks.

The five-month-long war between the Soviet Union and the Japanese 6th Army at *Khalkhin Gol* ([Nomonhan](/wiki/Nomonhan "Nomonhan")) in 1939 brought home some lessons[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*]. In this conflict, the Soviets fielded over two thousand tanks, to the around 73 cannon armed tanks deployed by the Japanese,[[52]](#cite_note-53) the major difference being that Japanese armour were equipped with [diesel](/wiki/Diesel_engine "Diesel engine") engines as opposed to the Russian tanks equipped with petrol engines.[[53]](#cite_note-54) After General [Georgy Zhukov](/wiki/Georgy_Zhukov "Georgy Zhukov") inflicted a defeat on the Japanese 6th Army with his massed combined tank and air attack, the Soviets learned a lesson on the use of gasoline engines, and quickly incorporated those newly found experiences into their new [T-34](/wiki/T-34 "T-34") medium tank during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[54]](#cite_note-55)

Prior to World War II, the tactics and strategy of deploying tank forces underwent a revolution. In August 1939, Soviet General [Georgy Zhukov](/wiki/Georgy_Zhukov "Georgy Zhukov") used the combined force of tanks and airpower at [Nomonhan](/wiki/Nomonhan "Nomonhan") against the Japanese 6th Army;[[55]](#cite_note-56) [Heinz Guderian](/wiki/Heinz_Guderian "Heinz Guderian"), a tactical theoretician who was heavily involved in the formation of the first independent German tank force, said "Where tanks are, the front is", and this concept became a reality in World War II.[[56]](#cite_note-57) Guderian's armoured warfare ideas, combined with Germany's existing doctrines of *Bewegungskrieg* ("[maneuver warfare](/wiki/Maneuver_warfare "Maneuver warfare")") and [infiltration tactics](/wiki/Infiltration_tactics "Infiltration tactics") from World War I, became the basis of [blitzkrieg](/wiki/Blitzkrieg "Blitzkrieg") in the opening stages of World War II.

#### World War II

Main article: [Tanks in World War II](/wiki/Tanks_in_World_War_II "Tanks in World War II")

##### Germany

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the first conflict in which armoured vehicles were critical to battlefield success, the tank and related tactics developed rapidly. Armoured forces proved capable of tactical victory in an unprecedentedly short amount of time, yet new [anti-tank](/wiki/Anti-tank "Anti-tank") weaponry showed that the tank was not invulnerable. During the Invasion of Poland, tanks performed in a more traditional role in close cooperation with infantry units, but in the [Battle of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France") deep independent armoured penetrations were executed by the Germans, a technique later called *blitzkrieg*. Blitzkrieg used innovative [combined arms](/wiki/Combined_arms "Combined arms") tactics and radios in all of the tanks to provide a level of tactical flexibility and power that surpassed that of the Allied armour. The [French Army](/wiki/French_Army "French Army"), with tanks equal or superior to the German tanks in both quality and quantity, employed a linear defensive strategy in which the armoured cavalry units were made subservient to the needs of the infantry armies to cover their entrenchment in Belgium.[[49]](#cite_note-Deighton1979-50) In addition, they lacked radios in many of their tanks and headquarters,[[57]](#cite_note-58) which limited their ability to respond to German attacks.

In accordance with blitzkrieg methods, German tanks bypassed enemy strongpoints and could radio for [close air support](/wiki/Close_air_support "Close air support") to destroy them, or leave them to the infantry. A related development, [motorized infantry](/wiki/Motorized_infantry "Motorized infantry"), allowed some of the troops to keep up with the tanks and create highly mobile combined arms forces.[[49]](#cite_note-Deighton1979-50) The defeat of a major military power within weeks shocked the rest of the world, spurring tank and anti-tank weapon development.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/M4A4_cutaway.svg/330px-M4A4_cutaway.svg.png)](/wiki/File:M4A4_cutaway.svg)

Cutaway of an [M4A4 Sherman](/wiki/M4_Sherman "M4 Sherman") tank, the primary tank used by the United States and a number of the other western allies during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War").

The [North African Campaign](/wiki/North_African_Campaign "North African Campaign") also provided an important battleground for tanks, as the flat, desolate terrain with relatively few obstacles or urban environments was ideal for conducting mobile armoured warfare. However, this battlefield also showed the importance of logistics, especially in an armoured force, as the principal warring armies, the German [Afrika Korps](/wiki/Afrika_Korps "Afrika Korps") and the [British Eighth Army](/wiki/British_Eighth_Army "British Eighth Army"), often outpaced their supply trains in repeated attacks and counter-attacks on each other, resulting in complete stalemate. This situation would not be resolved until 1942, when during the [Second Battle of El Alamein](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_El_Alamein "Second Battle of El Alamein"), the Afrika Korps, crippled by disruptions in their supply lines, had 95% of its tanks destroyed[[58]](#cite_note-59) and was forced to retreat by a massively reinforced [Eighth Army](/wiki/Eighth_Army_(United_Kingdom) "Eighth Army (United Kingdom)"), the first in a series of defeats that would eventually lead to the surrender of the remaining Axis forces in [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisian_Campaign "Tunisian Campaign").

##### Soviets

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101III-Merz-014-12A%2C_Russland%2C_Beginn_Unternehmen_Zitadelle%2C_Panzer.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101III-Merz-014-12A%2C_Russland%2C_Beginn_Unternehmen_Zitadelle%2C_Panzer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101III-Merz-014-12A,_Russland,_Beginn_Unternehmen_Zitadelle,_Panzer.jpg)

The [Battle of Kursk](/wiki/Battle_of_Kursk "Battle of Kursk") was credited to be the largest tank battle ever fought, with each side deploying nearly 3,000 tanks.

When Germany launched its invasion of the Soviet Union, [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa"), the Soviets had a superior tank design, the [T-34](/wiki/T-34 "T-34").[[59]](#cite_note-60) A lack of preparations for the [Axis](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") surprise attack, mechanical problems, poor training of the crews and incompetent leadership caused the Soviet machines to be surrounded and destroyed in large numbers. However, interference from [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler"),[[60]](#cite_note-61) the geographic scale of the conflict, the dogged resistance of the Soviet combat troops, and the Soviets' massive advantages in manpower and production capability prevented a repeat of the German successes of 1940.[[61]](#cite_note-62) Despite early successes against the Soviets, the Germans were forced to up-gun their Panzer IVs, and to design and build both the larger and more expensive [Tiger](/wiki/Tiger_I "Tiger I") heavy tank in 1942, and the [Panther](/wiki/Panther_tank "Panther tank") medium tank the following year. In doing so, the *Wehrmacht* denied the infantry and other support arms the production priorities that they needed to remain equal partners with the increasingly sophisticated tanks, in turn violating the principle of combined arms they had pioneered.[[22]](#cite_note-House1984-23) Soviet developments following the invasion included upgunning the T-34, development of self-propelled anti-tank guns such as the [SU-152](/wiki/SU-152 "SU-152"), and deployment of the [IS-2](/wiki/IS-2 "IS-2") in the closing stages of the war, with the T-34 being the most produced tank of World War II, totalling up to some 65,000 examples by May 1945.

##### United States

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Shermans_disembarking_from_LST_at_Anzio_crop.jpg/250px-Shermans_disembarking_from_LST_at_Anzio_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shermans_disembarking_from_LST_at_Anzio_crop.jpg)

[Sherman](/wiki/M4_Sherman "M4 Sherman") tanks joining the [U.S. Fifth Army](/wiki/U.S._Fifth_Army "U.S. Fifth Army") forces in the beachhead at [Anzio](/wiki/Anzio "Anzio") during the [Italian Campaign](/wiki/Italian_Campaign_(World_War_II) "Italian Campaign (World War II)"), 1944

Much like the Soviets, when entering World War II six months later (December 1941), the United States' [mass production](/wiki/Mass_production "Mass production") capacity enabled it to rapidly construct thousands of relatively cheap [M4 Sherman](/wiki/M4_Sherman "M4 Sherman") medium tanks. A compromise all round, the Sherman was reliable and formed a large part of the Anglo-American ground forces, but in a tank-versus-tank battle was no match for the Panther or Tiger.[[62]](#cite_note-63) Numerical and logistical superiority and the successful use of combined arms allowed the Allies to overrun the German forces during the [Battle of Normandy](/wiki/Invasion_of_Normandy "Invasion of Normandy"). Upgunned versions with the [76 mm gun M1](/wiki/76_mm_gun_M1 "76 mm gun M1") and the [17-pounder](/wiki/Sherman_Firefly "Sherman Firefly") were introduced to improve the M4's firepower, but concerns about protection remained—despite the apparent armour deficiencies, a total of some 42,000 Shermans were built and delivered to the Allied nations using it during the war years, a total second only to the T-34.

Tank [hulls](/wiki/Chassis "Chassis") were modified to produce [flame tanks](/wiki/Flame_tank "Flame tank"), mobile [rocket artillery](/wiki/Rocket_artillery "Rocket artillery"), and [combat engineering](/wiki/Combat_engineering "Combat engineering") vehicles for tasks including [mine-clearing](/wiki/Demining "Demining") and [bridging](/wiki/Bridge "Bridge").[[63]](#cite_note-64) Specialized self-propelled guns, most of which could double as [tank destroyers](/wiki/Tank_destroyer "Tank destroyer"), were also both developed by the Germans—with their *[Sturmgeschütz](/wiki/Sturmgesch%C3%BCtz "Sturmgeschütz")*, *[Panzerjäger](/wiki/Panzerj%C3%A4ger "Panzerjäger")* and *[Jagdpanzer](/wiki/Jagdpanzer "Jagdpanzer")* vehicles—and the *[Samokhodnaya ustanovka](/wiki/Samokhodnaya_ustanovka "Samokhodnaya ustanovka")* families of AFV's for the Soviets: such turretless, [casemate](/wiki/Casemate#Armoured_vehicles "Casemate")-style [tank destroyers](/wiki/Tank_destroyer "Tank destroyer") and [assault guns](/wiki/Assault_gun "Assault gun") were less complex, stripped down tanks carrying heavy guns, solely firing forward. The firepower and low cost of these vehicles made them attractive but as manufacturing techniques improved and larger turret rings made larger tank guns feasible, the [gun turret](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret") was recognized as the most effective mounting for the main gun to allow movement in a different direction from firing, enhancing tactical flexibility.[[49]](#cite_note-Deighton1979-50)

#### Cold War

Main article: [Tanks in the Cold War](/wiki/Tanks_in_the_Cold_War "Tanks in the Cold War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/T-72A_tank_on_parade.jpg/250px-T-72A_tank_on_parade.jpg)](/wiki/File:T-72A_tank_on_parade.jpg)

The Cold War era Soviet [T-72](/wiki/T-72 "T-72") was the most widely deployed main battle tank across the world.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), tension between the [Warsaw Pact](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact "Warsaw Pact") countries and North Atlantic Treaty Organization ([NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO")) countries created an [arms race](/wiki/Arms_race "Arms race") that ensured that tank development proceeded largely as it had during World War II. The essence of tank designs during the Cold War had been hammered out in the closing stages of World War II. Large turrets, capable suspension systems, greatly improved engines, [sloped armour](/wiki/Sloped_armour "Sloped armour") and large-caliber (90 mm and larger) guns were standard. Tank design during the Cold War built on this foundation and included improvements to [fire control](/wiki/Fire-control_system "Fire-control system"), [gyroscopic](/wiki/Gyroscopic "Gyroscopic") gun stabilization, communications (primarily radio) and crew comfort and saw the introduction of [laser](/wiki/Laser "Laser") rangefinders and [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") night vision equipment. [Armour technology](/wiki/Composite_armour "Composite armour") progressed in an ongoing race against improvements in [anti-tank weapons](/wiki/Anti-tank_weapons "Anti-tank weapons"), especially [antitank guided missiles](/wiki/Guided_missiles "Guided missiles") like the [TOW](/wiki/BGM-71_TOW "BGM-71 TOW").

Medium tanks of World War II evolved into the *[main battle tank](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank")* (MBT) of the Cold War and took over the majority of tank roles on the battlefield. This gradual transition occurred in the 1950s and 1960s due to [anti-tank](/wiki/Anti-tank "Anti-tank") [guided missiles](/wiki/Guided_missiles "Guided missiles"), [sabot](/wiki/Armour-piercing_discarding_sabot "Armour-piercing discarding sabot") ammunition and [high-explosive anti-tank](/wiki/High-explosive_anti-tank "High-explosive anti-tank") warheads. World War II had shown that the speed of a light tank was no substitute for armour & firepower and medium tanks were vulnerable to newer weapon technology, rendering them [obsolete](/wiki/Obsolete "Obsolete").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In a trend started in World War II, [economies of scale](/wiki/Economies_of_scale "Economies of scale") led to serial production of progressively upgraded models of all major tanks during the Cold War. For the same reason many upgraded post-World War II tanks and their derivatives (for example, the [T-55](/wiki/T-55 "T-55") and [T-72](/wiki/T-72 "T-72")) remain in active service around the world, and even an obsolete tank may be the most formidable weapon on battlefields in many parts of the world.[[65]](#cite_note-66) Among the tanks of the 1950s were the British [Centurion](/wiki/Centurion_Tank "Centurion Tank") and Soviet T-54/55 in service from 1946, and the US [M48](/wiki/M48_Patton "M48 Patton") from 1951.[[66]](#cite_note-67) These three vehicles formed the bulk of the armoured forces of NATO and the Warsaw Pact throughout much of the Cold War. Lessons learned from tanks such as the [Leopard 1](/wiki/Leopard_1 "Leopard 1"), [M48 Patton](/wiki/M48_Patton "M48 Patton") series, [Chieftain](/wiki/Chieftain_tank "Chieftain tank"), and T-72 led to the contemporary [Leopard 2](/wiki/Leopard_2 "Leopard 2"), [M1 Abrams](/wiki/M1_Abrams "M1 Abrams"), [Challenger 2](/wiki/Challenger_2_tank "Challenger 2 tank"), [C1 Ariete](/wiki/C1_Ariete "C1 Ariete"), [T-90](/wiki/T-90 "T-90") and [Merkava](/wiki/Merkava "Merkava") IV.

Tanks and anti-tank weapons of the Cold War era saw action in a number of [proxy wars](/wiki/Proxy_war "Proxy war") like the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), [Indo-Pakistani War of 1971](/wiki/Indo-Pakistani_War_of_1971 "Indo-Pakistani War of 1971"), [Soviet–Afghan War](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War") and Arab-Israeli conflicts, culminating with the [Yom Kippur War](/wiki/Yom_Kippur_War "Yom Kippur War"). The T-55, for example, has seen action in no fewer than [32 conflicts](/wiki/T-54/55#Other_conflicts "T-54/55"). In these wars the U.S. or NATO countries and the Soviet Union or China consistently backed opposing forces. Proxy wars were studied by Western and Soviet [military analysts](/wiki/Military_intelligence "Military intelligence") and provided a contribution to the Cold War tank development process.

### 21st century

Main article: [Tanks of the post–Cold War era](/wiki/Tanks_of_the_post%E2%80%93Cold_War_era "Tanks of the post–Cold War era")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Italian_Army_-_132nd_Tank_Regiment_Ariete_main_battle_tank_during_an_exercise_in_Italy_01.jpg/250px-Italian_Army_-_132nd_Tank_Regiment_Ariete_main_battle_tank_during_an_exercise_in_Italy_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italian_Army_-_132nd_Tank_Regiment_Ariete_main_battle_tank_during_an_exercise_in_Italy_01.jpg)

An Italian [C1 Ariete](/wiki/Ariete "Ariete") in 2022.

The role of tank vs. tank combat is becoming diminished. Tanks work in concert with infantry in urban warfare by deploying them ahead of the platoon. When engaging enemy infantry, tanks can provide covering fire on the battlefield. Conversely, tanks can spearhead attacks when infantry are deployed in personnel carriers.[[67]](#cite_note-Modern_tank_tactics,_infantry-68)

Tanks were used to spearhead the initial US invasion of Iraq in 2003. As of 2005, there were 1,100 [M1 Abrams](/wiki/M1_Abrams "M1 Abrams") used by the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") in the course of the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"), and they have proven to have an unexpectedly high level of vulnerability to [roadside bombs](/wiki/Roadside_bomb "Roadside bomb").[[68]](#cite_note-69) A relatively new type of remotely detonated mine, the [explosively formed penetrator](/wiki/Explosively_formed_penetrator "Explosively formed penetrator") has been used with some success against American armoured vehicles (particularly the [Bradley fighting vehicle](/wiki/Bradley_fighting_vehicle "Bradley fighting vehicle")). However, with upgrades to their armour in the rear, M1s have proven invaluable in fighting insurgents in urban combat, particularly at the [Battle of Fallujah](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Fallujah "Second Battle of Fallujah"), where the US Marines brought in two extra brigades.[[69]](#cite_note-USAToday2005b-70) Israeli [Merkava](/wiki/Merkava "Merkava") tanks contain features that enable them to support [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") in [low intensity conflicts](/wiki/Low_intensity_conflict "Low intensity conflict") (LIC) and [counter-terrorism](/wiki/Counter-terrorism "Counter-terrorism") operations. Such features are the rear door and rear corridor, enabling the tank to carry infantry and embark safely; the [IMI](/wiki/Israeli_Military_Industries "Israeli Military Industries") [APAM-MP-T](/w/index.php?title=APAM-MP-T&action=edit&redlink=1 "APAM-MP-T (page does not exist)") multi-purpose ammunition round, advanced [C4IS](/wiki/C4IS "C4IS") systems and recently: [TROPHY active protection system](/wiki/Trophy_(countermeasure) "Trophy (countermeasure)") which protects the tank from shoulder-launched anti-tank weapons. During the [Second Intifada](/wiki/Second_Intifada "Second Intifada") further modifications were made, designated as "Merkava Mk. 3d Baz LIC".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Research and development

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/XM1202_MCS.jpg/250px-XM1202_MCS.jpg)](/wiki/File:XM1202_MCS.jpg)

Graphic representation of the US Army's cancelled [XM1202 Mounted Combat System](/wiki/Future_Combat_Systems_manned_ground_vehicles#XM1202_Mounted_Combat_System "Future Combat Systems manned ground vehicles")

In terms of firepower, the focus of 2010s-era [R&D](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") was increased detection capability such as [thermal imagers](/wiki/Thermography "Thermography"), automated fire control systems for the guns and increased [muzzle energy](/wiki/Muzzle_energy "Muzzle energy") from the gun to improve range, accuracy and armour penetration.[[70]](#cite_note-71) The most mature future gun technology is the [electrothermal-chemical](/wiki/Electrothermal-chemical_technology "Electrothermal-chemical technology") gun.[[71]](#cite_note-72) The [XM291](/wiki/XM291 "XM291") electrothermal-chemical tank gun has gone through successful multiple firing sequences on a modified American [M8 Armored Gun System](/wiki/M8_Armored_Gun_System "M8 Armored Gun System") chassis.[[72]](#cite_note-73) To improve tank protection, one field of research involves making the tank invisible to radar by adapting [stealth](/wiki/Stealth_technology "Stealth technology") technologies originally designed for aircraft. Improvements to [camouflage](/wiki/Camouflage "Camouflage") or and attempts to render it invisible through [active camouflage](/wiki/Active_camouflage "Active camouflage"), which changes according to where the tank is located, are being pursued. Research is also ongoing in [electromagnetic](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism") armour systems to disperse or deflect incoming [shaped charges](/wiki/Hollow_charge "Hollow charge"),[[73]](#cite_note-74)[[74]](#cite_note-75) as well as various forms of [active protection systems](/wiki/Active_protection_system "Active protection system") to prevent incoming projectiles (RPGs, missiles, etc.) from striking the tank.

Mobility may be enhanced in future tanks by the use of [diesel–electric](/wiki/Diesel%E2%80%93electric_powertrain "Diesel–electric powertrain") or turbine–electric [series hybrid](/wiki/Series_hybrid "Series hybrid") drives—first used in a primitive, gasoline-engined form with Porsche's *[Elefant](/wiki/Elefant "Elefant")* German tank destroyer of 1943—improving fuel efficiency while reducing the size and weight of the power plant.[[75]](#cite_note-76) Furthermore, advances in gas turbine technology, including the use of advanced [recuperators](/wiki/Recuperator "Recuperator"),[[76]](#cite_note-77) have allowed for reduction in engine volume and mass to less than 1 m3 and 1 metric ton, respectively, while maintaining fuel efficiency similar to that of a diesel engine.[[77]](#cite_note-78) In line with the new doctrine of [network-centric warfare](/wiki/Network-centric_warfare "Network-centric warfare"), the 2010s-era modern battle tank shows increasing sophistication in its electronics and communication systems. The future of tanks has been challenged by the proliferation of relatively inexpensive anti tank guided missiles and rockets during the [Russo-Ukrainian War](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War").[[78]](#cite_note-79)

## Design

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/M1_Abrams-TUSK.svg/500px-M1_Abrams-TUSK.svg.png)](/wiki/File:M1_Abrams-TUSK.svg)

Labelled diagram of an M1 Abrams

The three traditional factors determining a tank's capability effectiveness are its *firepower*, *protection*, and *mobility*.[[79]](#cite_note-80)[[80]](#cite_note-81) Firepower is the ability of a tank's crew to identify, engage, and destroy enemy tanks and other targets using its large-caliber cannon. Protection is the degree to which the tank's armour, profile and camouflage enables the tank crew to evade detection, protect themselves from enemy fire, and retain vehicle functionality during and after combat. Mobility includes how well the tank can be transported by rail, sea, or air to the operational staging area; from the staging area by road or over terrain towards the enemy; and tactical movement by the tank over the battlefield during combat, including traversing of obstacles and rough terrain. The variations of tank designs have been determined by the way these three fundamental features are blended. For instance, in 1937, the French doctrine focused on firepower and protection more than mobility because tanks worked in intimate liaison with the infantry.[[81]](#cite_note-82) There was also the case of the development of a heavy cruiser tank, which focused on armour and firepower to challenge Germany's Tiger and Panther tanks.[[82]](#cite_note-83)

[The Kaplan/Harimau tank](/wiki/Kaplan_MT_/_Harimau "Kaplan MT / Harimau") made in [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") and [Türkiye](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") is a medium-quality tank programmed for areas that are difficult for large tanks to reach.

Tanks have been classified by weight, role, or other criteria, that has changed over time and place. Classification is determined by the prevailing theories of armoured warfare, which have been altered in turn by rapid advances in technology. No one classification system works across all periods or all nations; in particular, weight-based classification is inconsistent between countries and eras.

In World War I, the first tank designs focused on crossing wide trenches, requiring very long and large vehicles, such as the British Mark I; these became classified as [heavy tanks](/wiki/Heavy_tank "Heavy tank"). Tanks that fulfilled other combat roles were smaller, like the French Renault FT; these were classified as [light tanks](/wiki/Light_tank "Light tank") or [tankettes](/wiki/Tankette "Tankette"). Many late-war and inter-war tank designs diverged from these according to new, though mostly untried, concepts for future tank roles and tactics. Tank classifications varied considerably according to each nation's own tank development, such as "cavalry tanks", "fast tanks", and "breakthrough tanks".

During World War II, many tank concepts were found unsatisfactory and discarded, mostly leaving the more multi-role tanks; these became easier to classify. Tank classes based on weight (and the corresponding transport and logistical needs) led to new definitions of heavy and light tank classes, with [medium tanks](/wiki/Medium_tank "Medium tank") covering the balance of those between. The British maintained [cruiser tanks](/wiki/Cruiser_tank "Cruiser tank"), focused on speed, and [infantry tanks](/wiki/Infantry_tank "Infantry tank") that traded speed for more armour. [Tank destroyers](/wiki/Tank_destroyer "Tank destroyer") are tanks or other [armoured fighting vehicles](/wiki/Armoured_fighting_vehicle "Armoured fighting vehicle") specifically designed to defeat enemy tanks. [Assault guns](/wiki/Assault_gun "Assault gun") are armoured fighting vehicles that could combine the roles of infantry tanks and [tank destroyers](/wiki/Tank_destroyer "Tank destroyer"). Some tanks were converted to [flame tanks](/wiki/Flame_tank "Flame tank"), specializing on close-in attacks on enemy strongholds with [flamethrowers](/wiki/Flamethrower "Flamethrower"). As the war went on, tanks tended to become larger and more powerful, shifting some tank classifications and leading to [super-heavy tanks](/wiki/Super-heavy_tank "Super-heavy tank").

Experience and technology advances during the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") continued to consolidate tank roles. With the worldwide adoption of the modern [main battle tank](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank") designs, which favour a modular universal design, most other classifications are dropped from modern terminology. All main battle tanks tend to have a good balance of speed, armour, and firepower, even while technology continues to improve all three. Being fairly large, main battle tanks can be complemented with light tanks, [armoured personnel carriers](/wiki/Armoured_personnel_carrier "Armoured personnel carrier"), [infantry fighting vehicles](/wiki/Infantry_fighting_vehicle "Infantry fighting vehicle") or similar relatively lighter armoured fighting vehicles, typically in the roles of [armoured reconnaissance](/wiki/Armoured_reconnaissance "Armoured reconnaissance"), [amphibious](/wiki/Amphibious_warfare "Amphibious warfare") or [air assault](/wiki/Air_assault "Air assault") operations, or against enemies lacking main battle tanks.

### Offensive capabilities

Main article: [Tank gun](/wiki/Tank_gun "Tank gun")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/105mm_tank_gun_Rifling.jpg/250px-105mm_tank_gun_Rifling.jpg)](/wiki/File:105mm_tank_gun_Rifling.jpg)

A sectioned 105mm [rifled](/wiki/Rifling "Rifling") [Royal Ordnance L7](/wiki/Royal_Ordnance_L7 "Royal Ordnance L7") tank gun

The [main weapon](/wiki/Tank_gun "Tank gun") of modern tanks is typically a single, large-[caliber](/wiki/Caliber "Caliber") [cannon](/wiki/Cannon "Cannon") mounted in a [fully traversing](/wiki/Gun_laying "Gun laying") (rotating) [gun turret](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret"). The typical modern tank gun is a [smoothbore](/wiki/Smoothbore "Smoothbore") weapon capable of firing a variety of ammunition, including [armour-piercing](/wiki/Armour-piercing "Armour-piercing") [kinetic energy penetrators](/wiki/Kinetic_energy_penetrator "Kinetic energy penetrator") (KEP), also known as [armour-piercing discarding sabot](/wiki/Armour-piercing_discarding_sabot "Armour-piercing discarding sabot") (APDS), and/or [armour-piercing fin-stabilized discarding sabot](/wiki/APFSDS "APFSDS") (APFSDS) and [high-explosive anti-tank](/wiki/High-explosive_anti-tank "High-explosive anti-tank") (HEAT) [shells](/wiki/Shell_(projectile) "Shell (projectile)"), and/or [high-explosive squash head](/wiki/High-explosive_squash_head "High-explosive squash head") (HESH) and/or [anti-tank guided missiles](/wiki/Anti-tank_guided_missile "Anti-tank guided missile") (ATGM) to destroy armoured targets, as well as [high-explosive](/wiki/High-explosive_fragmentation "High-explosive fragmentation") (HE) [shells](/wiki/High-explosive_fragmentation "High-explosive fragmentation") for shooting at "soft" targets (unarmoured vehicles or troops) or [fortifications](/wiki/Fortifications "Fortifications"). [Canister shot](/wiki/Canister_shot "Canister shot") may be used in close or urban combat situations where the risk of hitting friendly forces with [shrapnel](/wiki/Shrapnel_(fragment) "Shrapnel (fragment)") from HE rounds is unacceptably high.[[69]](#cite_note-USAToday2005b-70)

A [gyroscope](/wiki/Gyroscope "Gyroscope") is used to stabilise the main gun, allowing it to be effectively aimed and fired at the "short halt" or on the move. Modern tank guns are also commonly fitted with [insulating](/wiki/Thermal_insulation "Thermal insulation") [thermal sleeves](/wiki/Thermal_sleeve "Thermal sleeve") to reduce gun-barrel warping caused by uneven [thermal expansion](/wiki/Thermal_expansion "Thermal expansion"), [bore evacuators](/wiki/Bore_evacuator "Bore evacuator") to minimise gun firing fumes entering the crew compartment and sometimes [muzzle brakes](/wiki/Muzzle_brake "Muzzle brake") to minimise the effect of [recoil](/wiki/Recoil "Recoil") on accuracy and [rate of fire](/wiki/Rate_of_fire "Rate of fire").

Traditionally, target detection relied on visual identification. This was accomplished from within the tank through [telescopic](/wiki/Telescope "Telescope") [periscopes](/wiki/Periscope "Periscope"); often, however, tank commanders would open up the hatch to view the outside surroundings, which improved situational awareness but incurred the penalty of vulnerability to sniper fire. Though several developments in target detection have taken place, these methods are still common practice. In the 2010s, more electronic target detection methods are available.

In some cases [spotting rifles](/wiki/Spotting_rifle "Spotting rifle") were used to confirm proper trajectory and range to a target. These spotting rifles were mounted co-axially to the main gun, and fired [tracer ammunition](/wiki/Tracer_ammunition "Tracer ammunition") ballistically matched to the gun itself. The gunner would track the movement of the tracer round in flight, and upon impact with a hard surface, it would give off a flash and a puff of smoke, after which the main gun was immediately fired. However this slow method has been mostly superseded by [laser rangefinding](/wiki/Laser_rangefinder "Laser rangefinder") equipment.

Modern tanks also use sophisticated [light intensification](/wiki/Night_vision_device "Night vision device") and [thermal imaging](/wiki/Thermal_imaging "Thermal imaging") equipment to improve fighting capability at night, in poor weather and in smoke. The accuracy of modern tank guns is pushed to the mechanical limit by computerized [fire-control systems](/wiki/Fire-control_system "Fire-control system"). A fire-control system uses a laser rangefinder to determine the range to the target, a [thermocouple](/wiki/Thermocouple "Thermocouple"), [anemometer](/wiki/Anemometer "Anemometer") and [wind vane](/wiki/Wind_vane "Wind vane") to correct for weather effects and a muzzle referencing system to correct for gun-barrel temperature, warping and wear. Two sightings of a target with the range-finder enable calculation of the target movement [vector](/wiki/Vector_(geometric) "Vector (geometric)"). This information is combined with the known movement of the tank and the principles of [ballistics](/wiki/External_ballistics "External ballistics") to calculate the [elevation](/wiki/Elevation_(ballistics) "Elevation (ballistics)") and [aim point](/wiki/Aiming_point "Aiming point") that maximises the probability of hitting the target.

Usually, tanks carry smaller caliber armament for short-range defense where fire from the main weapon would be ineffective or wasteful, for example when engaging [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry"), [light vehicles](/wiki/Armoured_car_(military) "Armoured car (military)") or [close air support](/wiki/Close_air_support "Close air support") aircraft. A typical complement of secondary weapons is a general-purpose machine gun mounted [coaxially](/wiki/Coaxial_weapon "Coaxial weapon") with the main gun, and a heavier [anti-aircraft](/wiki/Anti-aircraft "Anti-aircraft")-capable machine gun on the turret roof. Some tanks also have a hull-mounted machine gun. These weapons are often modified variants of those used by infantry, and so use the same kinds of ammunition.

### Protection and countermeasures

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/T-90_tank_during_the_Victory_Day_parade_in_2009.jpg/250px-T-90_tank_during_the_Victory_Day_parade_in_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:T-90_tank_during_the_Victory_Day_parade_in_2009.jpg)

The Russian [T-90](/wiki/T-90 "T-90") is fitted with a "three-tiered" protection systems:  
1: [Composite armour](/wiki/Composite_armour "Composite armour") in the turret  
 2: Third generation [Kontakt-5](/wiki/Kontakt-5 "Kontakt-5") [ERA](/wiki/Explosive_reactive_armour "Explosive reactive armour")  
 3: [Shtora](/wiki/Shtora "Shtora")-1 countermeasures suite.

See also: [Anti-tank warfare](/wiki/Anti-tank_warfare "Anti-tank warfare")

The measure of a tank's protection is the combination of its ability to avoid detection (due to having a low profile and through the use of camouflage), to avoid being hit by enemy fire, its resistance to the effects of enemy fire, and its capacity to sustain damage whilst still completing its objective, or at least protecting its crew. This is done by a variety of countermeasures, such as armour plating and reactive defenses, as well as more complex ones such as heat-emissions reduction.

In common with most unit types, tanks are subject to additional hazards in dense wooded and urban combat environments which largely negate the advantages of the tank's long-range firepower and mobility, limit the crew's detection capabilities and can restrict turret traverse. Despite these disadvantages, tanks retain high [survivability](/wiki/Survivability "Survivability") against previous-generation [rocket-propelled grenades](/wiki/Rocket-propelled_grenade "Rocket-propelled grenade") aimed at the most-armoured sections.

However, as effective and advanced as armour plating has become, tank survivability against newer-generation [tandem-warhead](/wiki/Tandem-warhead "Tandem-warhead") anti-tank missiles is a concern for military planners.[[83]](#cite_note-84) Tandem-warhead RPGs use two warheads to fool active protection systems; a first dummy warhead is fired first, to trigger the active defenses, with the real warhead following it. For example, the [RPG-29](/wiki/RPG-29 "RPG-29") from the 1980s is able to penetrate the frontal hull armour of the Challenger II[[84]](#cite_note-85) and also managed to damage a M1 Abrams.[[85]](#cite_note-86) As well, even tanks with advanced armour plating can have their tracks or gear cogs damaged by RPGs, which may render them immobile or hinder their mobility. Despite all of the advances in armour plating, a tank with its hatches open remains vulnerable to [Molotov cocktail](/wiki/Molotov_cocktail "Molotov cocktail") (gasoline bombs) and grenades. Even a "buttoned up" tank may have components which are vulnerable to Molotov cocktails, such as optics, extra gas cans and extra ammunition stored on the outside of the tank.

#### Avoiding detection

Further information: [Military deception](/wiki/Military_deception "Military deception")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Type_99_MBT_front_right.jpg/250px-Type_99_MBT_front_right.jpg)](/wiki/File:Type_99_MBT_front_right.jpg)

[PLA](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army")'s [Type 99a tank](/wiki/Type_99a_tank "Type 99a tank") with [disruptive](/wiki/Disruptive_coloration "Disruptive coloration") camouflage painting

A tank avoids detection using the doctrine of [countermeasures](/wiki/Countermeasure "Countermeasure") known as CCD: [camouflage](/wiki/Military_camouflage "Military camouflage") (looks the same as the surroundings), concealment (cannot be seen) and deception (looks like something else).

##### Camouflage

Further information: [Military camouflage](/wiki/Military_camouflage "Military camouflage")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Challenger_2-Megatron_MOD_45161486.jpg/250px-Challenger_2-Megatron_MOD_45161486.jpg)](/wiki/File:Challenger_2-Megatron_MOD_45161486.jpg)

A British [Challenger 2](/wiki/Challenger_2 "Challenger 2") Theatre Entry Standard fitted with a mobile camouflage system.

Camouflage can include disruptive painted shapes on the tank to break up the distinctive appearance and silhouette of a tank. Netting or actual branches from the surrounding landscape are also used. Prior to development of infrared technology, tanks were often given a coating of camouflage paint that, depending on environmental region or season, would allow it to blend in with the rest of its environment. A tank operating in wooded areas would typically get a green and brown paintjob; a tank in a winter environment would get white paint (often mixed with some darker colors); tanks in the desert often get khaki paintjobs.

The Russian [Nakidka](/wiki/Nakidka "Nakidka") camouflage kit was designed to reduce the [optical](/wiki/Optical "Optical"), [thermal](/wiki/Thermal_radiation "Thermal radiation"), [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared"), and [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") signatures of a tank, so that acquisition of the tank would be difficult. According to Nii Stali, the designers of Nakidka, Nakidka would reduce the probabilities of detection via "visual and near-IR bands by 30%, the thermal band by 2–3-fold, radar band by 6-fold, and radar-thermal band to near-background levels.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

##### Concealment

|  |  |

| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**.
