Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(November 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Concealment can include hiding the tank among trees or digging in the tank by having a combat bulldozer dig out part of a hill, so that much of the tank will be hidden. A tank commander can conceal the tank by using "hull down" approaches to going over upward-sloping hills, so that she or he can look out the commander's cupola without the distinctive-looking main cannon cresting over the hill. Adopting a turret-down or [hull-down](/wiki/Hull-down "Hull-down") position reduces the visible silhouette of a tank as well as providing the added protection of a position in [defilade](/wiki/Enfilade_and_defilade#Defilade "Enfilade and defilade").

Working against efforts to avoid detection is the fact that a tank is a large metallic object with a distinctive, angular [silhouette](/wiki/Silhouette "Silhouette") that emits copious [heat](/wiki/Thermal_imaging "Thermal imaging") and engine noise. A tank that is operating in cold weather or which needs to use its radio or other communications or target-detecting electronics will need to start its engine regularly to maintain its battery power, which will create engine noise. Consequently, it is difficult to effectively camouflage a tank in the absence of some form of cover or concealment (e.g., woods) it can [hide](/wiki/Hull-down "Hull-down") its hull behind. The tank becomes easier to detect when moving (typically, whenever it is in use) due to the large, distinctive auditory, vibration and thermal signature of its engine and power plant. Tank tracks and dust clouds also betray past or present tank movement.

Switched-off tanks are vulnerable to infra-red [detection](/wiki/Thermal_imaging "Thermal imaging") due to differences between the [thermal conductivity](/wiki/Thermal_conductivity "Thermal conductivity") and therefore [heat dissipation](/wiki/Heat#Heat_dissipation "Heat") of the metallic tank and its surroundings. At close range the tank can be detected even when powered down and fully concealed due to the [column of warmer air](/wiki/Heat_haze "Heat haze") above the tank and the smell of diesel or gasoline. Thermal blankets slow the rate of heat emission and some thermal camouflage nets use a mix of materials with differing thermal properties to operate in the infra-red as well as the [visible spectrum](/wiki/Visible_spectrum "Visible spectrum").

[Grenade launchers](/wiki/Grenade_launcher "Grenade launcher") can rapidly deploy a [smoke screen](/wiki/Smoke_screen "Smoke screen") that is opaque to [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") light, to hide it from the thermal viewer of another tank. In addition to using its own grenade launchers, a tank commander could call in an artillery unit to provide smoke cover. Some tanks can produce a smoke screen.

Sometimes camouflage and concealment are used at the same time. For example, a camouflage-painted and branch-covered tank (camouflage) may be hidden in a behind a hill or in a dug-in-emplacement (concealment).

##### Deception

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/E04934.jpg/250px-E04934.jpg)](/wiki/File:E04934.jpg)

Troops carry a lightweight wood-framed "dummy" tank into position.

Some [armoured recovery vehicles](/wiki/Armoured_recovery_vehicle "Armoured recovery vehicle") (often tracked, tank chassis-based "tow trucks" for tanks) have dummy turrets and cannons. This makes it less likely that enemy tanks will fire on these vehicles. Some armies have fake "dummy" tanks made of wood which troops can carry into position and hide behind obstacles. These "dummy" tanks may cause the enemy to think that there are more tanks than are actually possessed.

To effectively protect the tank and its crew, tank armour must counter a wide variety of antitank threats. Protection against [kinetic energy penetrators](/wiki/Kinetic_energy_penetrator "Kinetic energy penetrator") and [high-explosive anti-tank](/wiki/High-explosive_anti-tank "High-explosive anti-tank") (HEAT) shells fired by other tanks is of primary importance, but tank armour also aims to protect against infantry [mortars](/wiki/Mortar_(weapon) "Mortar (weapon)"), [grenades](/wiki/Grenades "Grenades"), [rocket-propelled grenades](/wiki/Rocket-propelled_grenade "Rocket-propelled grenade"), [anti-tank guided missiles](/wiki/Anti-tank_guided_missile "Anti-tank guided missile"), [anti-tank mines](/wiki/Land_mine "Land mine"), [anti-tank rifles](/wiki/Anti-tank_rifles "Anti-tank rifles"), [bombs](/wiki/Bomb "Bomb"), direct [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") hits, and (less often) [nuclear, biological and chemical](/wiki/Weapon_of_mass_destruction "Weapon of mass destruction") threats, any of which could disable or destroy a tank or its crew.

[Steel armour plate](/wiki/Rolled_homogeneous_armour "Rolled homogeneous armour") was the earliest type of armour. The Germans pioneered the use of [face hardened](/wiki/Carbon_steel#Case_hardening "Carbon steel") steel during World War II and the Soviets also achieved improved protection with [sloped armour](/wiki/Sloped_armour "Sloped armour") technology. World War II developments led to the obsolescence of homogeneous steel armour with the development of [shaped-charge](/wiki/Shaped-charge "Shaped-charge") warheads, exemplified by the [Panzerfaust](/wiki/Panzerfaust "Panzerfaust") and [bazooka](/wiki/Bazooka "Bazooka") infantry-carried weapons which were effective, despite some early success with [spaced armour](/wiki/Spaced_armour "Spaced armour"). Magnetic mines led to the development of [anti-magnetic](/wiki/Diamagnetism "Diamagnetism") paste and paint. From WWII to the modern era, troops have added improvised armour to tanks while in combat settings, such as sandbags or pieces of old armour plating.

British tank researchers took the next step with the development of [Chobham armour](/wiki/Chobham_armour "Chobham armour"), or more generally [composite armour](/wiki/Composite_armour "Composite armour"), incorporating [ceramics](/wiki/Ceramic "Ceramic") and plastics in a [resin](/wiki/Resin "Resin") matrix between steel plates, which provided good protection against HEAT weapons. [High-explosive squash head](/wiki/High-explosive_squash_head "High-explosive squash head") warheads led to [anti-spall](/wiki/Spall "Spall") armour linings, and kinetic energy penetrators led to the inclusion of exotic materials like a matrix of [depleted uranium](/wiki/Depleted_uranium "Depleted uranium") into a composite armour configuration.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/M60A1-Patton-Blazer-latrun-2.jpg/250px-M60A1-Patton-Blazer-latrun-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:M60A1-Patton-Blazer-latrun-2.jpg)

Blazer [explosive reactive armour](/wiki/Explosive_reactive_armour "Explosive reactive armour") (ERA) blocks on an Israeli M-60

[Reactive armour](/wiki/Reactive_armour "Reactive armour") consists of small explosive-filled metal boxes that detonate when hit by the metallic jet projected by an exploding HEAT warhead, causing their metal plates to disrupt it. [Tandem warheads](/wiki/Tandem_warhead "Tandem warhead") defeat reactive armour by causing the armour to detonate prematurely. Modern reactive armour protects itself from Tandem warheads by having a thicker front metal plate to prevent the precursor charge from detonating the explosive in the reactive armour. Reactive armours can also reduce the penetrative abilities of [kinetic energy penetrators](/wiki/Kinetic_energy_penetrator "Kinetic energy penetrator") by deforming the penetrator with the metal plates on the Reactive armour, thereby reducing its effectiveness against the main armour of the tank.

#### Active protection system

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Flickr_-_Israel_Defense_Forces_-_Storming_Ahead.jpg/250px-Flickr_-_Israel_Defense_Forces_-_Storming_Ahead.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_Israel_Defense_Forces_-_Storming_Ahead.jpg)

[IDF](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") [Merkava](/wiki/Merkava "Merkava") Mk4 tank with [Trophy](/wiki/Trophy_(countermeasure) "Trophy (countermeasure)") [APS](/wiki/Active_protection_system "Active protection system") ("מעיל רוח") during training

The latest generation of protective measures for tanks are [active protection systems](/wiki/Active_protection_system "Active protection system"). The term "active" is used to contrast these approaches with the armour used as the primary protective approach in earlier tanks.

* [Soft kill](/wiki/Soft_kill "Soft kill") measures, such as the Russian [Shtora](/wiki/Shtora "Shtora") countermeasure system, provide protection by interfering with enemy targeting and fire-control systems, thus making it harder for the enemy threats to lock onto the targeted tank.
* Hard kill systems intercept incoming threats with a projectile(s) of its own, destroying the threat. For example, the Israeli [Trophy](/wiki/Trophy_(countermeasure) "Trophy (countermeasure)") destroys an incoming rocket or missile with shotgun-like projectiles. The Soviet [Drozd](/wiki/Drozd "Drozd"), the Russian [Arena](/wiki/Arena_active_protection_system "Arena active protection system"), the Israeli [Trophy](/wiki/Trophy_(countermeasure) "Trophy (countermeasure)") and [Iron Fist](/wiki/Iron_Fist_(countermeasure) "Iron Fist (countermeasure)"), Polish [ERAWA](/w/index.php?title=ERAWA&action=edit&redlink=1 "ERAWA (page does not exist)"), and the American [Quick Kill](/wiki/Quick_Kill "Quick Kill") systems show the potential to dramatically improve protection for tanks against [missiles](/wiki/Anti-tank_missile "Anti-tank missile"), [RPGs](/wiki/Rocket-propelled_grenade "Rocket-propelled grenade") and potentially kinetic energy penetrator attacks, but concerns regarding a [danger zone for nearby troops](/wiki/Friendly_fire "Friendly fire") remain.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Mobility

Two [German Army](/wiki/German_Army "German Army") Leopard 2s demonstrate their [deep-wading](/wiki/Amphibious_vehicle#Deep_fording "Amphibious vehicle") capabilities

The mobility of a tank is described by its battlefield or tactical mobility, its operational mobility, and its strategic mobility.[[87]](#cite_note-logistics_burden-88)

* **Tactical mobility** is the tank's ability to move through the battle area. This could include acceleration, braking, speed and rate of turn on varying terrain, and obstacle clearance: the tank's ability to travel over or through obstacles like walls, trenches, and water.
* **Operational mobility** is the ability to move tanks hundreds of kilometers from a staging area to the battle area, for example, by using transport helicopters.
* **Strategic mobility** is the ability of the tanks to be transported over long distances, usually by air or sea. For tanks to be transported efficiently by air, weight and volume must be kept within the transport aircraft's capabilities.[[87]](#cite_note-logistics_burden-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/M1_strategic_mobility.jpg/250px-M1_strategic_mobility.jpg)](/wiki/File:M1_strategic_mobility.jpg)

[M1 Abrams](/wiki/M1_Abrams "M1 Abrams") offloading from [Landing Craft Air Cushioned](/wiki/Landing_Craft_Air_Cushioned "Landing Craft Air Cushioned") vehicle.

Tanks have high tactical mobility and can travel over most types of terrain due to their [continuous tracks](/wiki/Continuous_track "Continuous track") and advanced suspension. The tracks disperse the weight of the vehicle over a large area, resulting in less [ground pressure](/wiki/Ground_pressure "Ground pressure"). A tank can travel at approximately 40 kilometres per hour (25 mph) across flat terrain and up to 70 kilometres per hour (43 mph) on roads, but due to the mechanical strain this places on the vehicle and the logistical strain on fuel delivery and tank maintenance, these must be considered exceptional "burst" speeds.

Tanks are susceptible to mechanical failure of engine and transmission systems, particularly at maximum burst speeds. Consequently, wheeled tank transporters and rail transport are used wherever possible for non-combat tank transport. Tank mobility is very restricted compared to wheeled [armoured fighting vehicles](/wiki/Armoured_fighting_vehicle "Armoured fighting vehicle"). Most operational mobility in blitzkrieg tank operations was conducted at the pedestrian pace of 5 kilometres per hour (3.1 mph), and that was only achieved on the roads of France.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/M88_pulling_M1_engine.jpg/250px-M88_pulling_M1_engine.jpg)](/wiki/File:M88_pulling_M1_engine.jpg)

The [M1 Abrams](/wiki/M1_Abrams "M1 Abrams") is powered by a 1,500 shaft horsepower (1,100 kW) [Honeywell](/wiki/Honeywell "Honeywell") [AGT 1500](/wiki/AGT-1500 "AGT-1500") gas turbine engine, giving it a governed top speed of 45 mph (72 km/h) on paved roads, and 30 mph (48 km/h) cross-country.

### Suspension and running gear

Tank agility is a function of the weight of the tank due to its inertia while manoeuvring and its [ground pressure](/wiki/Ground_pressure "Ground pressure"), the power output of the installed power plant and the tank [transmission](/wiki/Transmission_(mechanics) "Transmission (mechanics)") and [track](/wiki/Continuous_track "Continuous track") design. In addition, rough terrain effectively limits the tank's speed through the stress it puts on the [suspension](/wiki/Suspension_(vehicle)#Armoured_fighting_vehicle_suspension "Suspension (vehicle)") and the crew. A breakthrough in this area was achieved during World War II when improved suspension systems were developed that allowed better cross-country performance and limited firing on the move. Systems like the earlier [Christie](/wiki/Christie_suspension "Christie suspension") or later [torsion-bar](/wiki/Torsion-bar "Torsion-bar") suspension developed by [Ferdinand Porsche](/wiki/Ferdinand_Porsche "Ferdinand Porsche") dramatically improved the tank's cross-country performance and overall mobility.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

### Engine

The tank's power plant supplies [kinetic energy](/wiki/Kinetic_energy "Kinetic energy") to move the tank, and [electric](/wiki/Electric_current "Electric current") power via a [generator](/wiki/Electrical_generator#Vehicle-mounted_generators "Electrical generator") to components such as the [turret](/wiki/Gun_turret "Gun turret") rotation motors and the tank's electronic systems.

The tank power plant evolved from predominantly petrol and adapted large-displacement aeronautical or automotive [engines](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engines "Internal combustion engines") to [diesel engines](/wiki/Diesel_engine "Diesel engine"). Japan was the first to begin transitioning to this engine type beginning with the [Type 89B](/wiki/Type_89B "Type 89B") in 1934. The main advantage of diesel is their higher [fuel economy](/wiki/Fuel_efficiency "Fuel efficiency"), which allows for greater operating ranges. Diesel engines can also run on a variety of fuels, such as aviation kerosene and even gasoline. Advanced [multi-fuel](/wiki/Multi-fuel "Multi-fuel") [diesel engines](/wiki/Diesel_engine#Quality_and_variety_of_fuels "Diesel engine") have been adopted.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOgorkiewicz1991247-249-91) [Gas turbines](/wiki/Gas_turbine#Tanks "Gas turbine") are powerful per unit weight but fuel-hungry; they have been used in a few tanks, including the Soviet [T-80](/wiki/T-80 "T-80") and American [M1 Abrams](/wiki/M1_Abrams "M1 Abrams").

### Fording

In the absence of [combat engineers](/wiki/Combat_engineer "Combat engineer"), most tanks can only [ford](/wiki/Ford_(crossing) "Ford (crossing)") small rivers. The typical fording depth for MBTs is approximately 1 m (3.3 ft), being limited by the height of the engine air intake and driver's position. Modern tanks such as the Russian [T-90](/wiki/T-90 "T-90") and the German [Leopard 1](/wiki/Leopard_1 "Leopard 1") and [Leopard 2](/wiki/Leopard_2 "Leopard 2") tanks can ford to a depth of 3 to 4 m (9.8 to 13.1 ft) when properly prepared and equipped with a [snorkel](/wiki/Vehicle_snorkel "Vehicle snorkel") to supply air for the crew and engine. Tank crews usually strongly dislike deep fording, but it adds considerable scope for [surprise](/wiki/List_of_military_tactics "List of military tactics") and tactical flexibility in water-crossing operations by opening new and unexpected avenues of attack.

[Amphibious tanks](/wiki/Amphibious_tank "Amphibious tank") are specially designed or adapted for water operations, such as by including snorkels and skirts, but they are rare in modern armies. Purpose-built [amphibious assault vehicles](/wiki/Amphibious_assault_vehicle "Amphibious assault vehicle") or [armoured personnel carriers](/wiki/Armoured_personnel_carrier "Armoured personnel carrier") are used, without tanks, in [amphibious assaults](/wiki/Amphibious_warfare#Post–World_War_II "Amphibious warfare"). Advances such as the [EFA mobile bridge](/wiki/EFA_(mobile_bridge) "EFA (mobile bridge)") and [armoured vehicle-launched scissors bridges](/wiki/Armoured_vehicle-launched_bridge "Armoured vehicle-launched bridge") have also reduced the impediment to tank advance that rivers posed in World War II.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

Most modern tanks most often have four crew members, or three if an [auto-loader](/wiki/Auto-loader "Auto-loader") is installed. These are the:

* [Commander](/wiki/Commanding_officer "Commanding officer") – The commander is responsible for commanding the tank, with all-round vision devices rather than the limited vision of the driver and gunner. He guides the gunner roughly onto target and guides the driver around turns and obstacles.
* [Gunner](/wiki/Gunner_(artillery) "Gunner (artillery)") – The gunner is responsible for [laying (aiming) the gun](/wiki/Gun_laying "Gun laying"). It may be laying for direct fire, where the gun is aimed similarly to a rifle, or indirect fire, where firing data is calculated and applied to the sights. The term includes automated aiming using, for example, radar-derived target data and computer-controlled guns. Gun laying involves moving the axis of the bore of the barrel in two planes, horizontal and vertical. A gun is "traversed" (rotated in a horizontal plane) to align it with the target, and "elevated" (moved in the vertical plane) to range it to the target.
* Loader – The loader loads the gun, with a round appropriate to the target (HEAT, smoke, etc.) as ordered by either the commander or the gunner. The loader is usually the lowest-ranked member of the crew. In tanks with auto-loaders this position is omitted.
* [Driver](/wiki/Driver_(person) "Driver (person)") – The driver drives the tank, and also performs routine maintenance on the automotive features.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/3rd_ID_M1A1_Abrams_TC_and_Gunner_2008.jpg/250px-3rd_ID_M1A1_Abrams_TC_and_Gunner_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:3rd_ID_M1A1_Abrams_TC_and_Gunner_2008.jpg)

A view in a M1A1 Abrams tank of the gunner's station (bottom left) and commander's station (top right)

Operating a tank is a team effort. For example, the loader is assisted by the rest of the crew in stowing ammunition. The driver is assisted in maintaining the automotive features.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

Historically, crews have varied from two to twelve members. First World War tanks carried the crew needed to man the multiple guns and machine guns, and up to four crewmen to drive the tank: the commander drove the tank and manned the brakes, steering via orders to his gears-men; a co-driver operated the gearbox and throttle; and two gears-men, one for each track, steered by setting their side to idle, allowing the track on the other side to slew the tank to one side. Pre-World War II French tanks were noted for having a two-man crew, in which the overworked commander had to load and fire the gun in addition to commanding the tank.

With World War II the multi-turreted tanks proved impracticable, and as the single turret on a low hull design became standard, crews became standardized around a crew of four or five. In those tanks with a fifth crew member, usually three were located in the turret (as described above) while the fifth was most often seated in the hull next to the driver, and operated the hull machine gun in addition to acting as a co-driver or radio operator. Well-designed crew stations, giving proper consideration to comfort and ergonomics, are important to the combat-effectiveness of a tank, as they limit fatigue and speed up individual actions.

### Engineering constraints

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Arjun_MBT_bump_track_test.JPG/250px-Arjun_MBT_bump_track_test.JPG)](/wiki/File:Arjun_MBT_bump_track_test.JPG)

The Indian [Arjun MBT](/wiki/Arjun_MBT "Arjun MBT")'s [hydropneumatic suspension](/wiki/Hydropneumatic_suspension "Hydropneumatic suspension") at work, while moving over a bump track.

A noted author on the subject of tank design engineering, [Richard Ogorkiewicz](/wiki/Richard_Ogorkiewicz "Richard Ogorkiewicz"), outlined the following basic engineering sub-systems that are commonly incorporated into a tank's technological development:[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOgorkiewicz1991-94)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

* Mobility (through chassis design)
* [Engines](/wiki/Engine#Combustion_engine "Engine")
* [Transmissions](/wiki/Transmission_(mechanics) "Transmission (mechanics)")
* [Suspensions](/wiki/Suspension_(vehicle) "Suspension (vehicle)") and [running gear](/wiki/Running_gear "Running gear")
* Soil-vehicle mechanics
* [Guns](/wiki/Tank_gun "Tank gun") and [ammunition](/wiki/Ammunition#Ordnance_ammunition "Ammunition")
* [Ballistics](/wiki/Ballistics "Ballistics") and mechanics of guns
* Vision and sighting systems
* Illuminating and [night vision](/wiki/Night_Vision#Night_vision_technologies "Night Vision") systems
* [Fire control systems](/wiki/Fire-control_system "Fire-control system") for main and auxiliary weapons
* [Gun control systems](/wiki/Gun_data_computer "Gun data computer")
* [Guided weapons](/wiki/Precision-guided_munition "Precision-guided munition")
* [Armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour#Armoured_fighting_vehicles "Vehicle armour") protection
* Configuration

To the above can be added unit communication systems and electronic anti-tank countermeasures, crew ergonomic and survival systems (including flame suppression), and provision for technological upgrading.
Few tank designs have survived their entire service lives without some upgrading or modernization, particularly during wartime, including some that have changed almost beyond recognition, such as the latest Israeli [Magach](/wiki/Magach#Versions "Magach") versions.

The characteristics of a tank are determined by the performance criteria required for the tank. The obstacles that must be traversed affect the vehicle's front and rear profiles. The types of terrain specified to be traversed determine the maximum permissible track ground pressure.[[94]](#cite_note-performance_reqs-95)

Tank design is a compromise between technological and budgetary constraints and tactical capability requirements. It is not possible to maximise firepower, protection and mobility simultaneously, while also incorporating the latest technology and being economically viable.
For example, in the case of tactical capability requirements, increasing protection by adding armour will result in an increase in weight and therefore decrease in mobility; increasing firepower by installing a larger gun will force the designer team to increase armour, the therefore weight of the tank by retaining same internal volume to ensure crew efficiency during combat. In the case of the Abrams MBT which has good firepower, speed and armour, these advantages are counterbalanced by its engine's notably high fuel consumption, which ultimately reduces its range, and in a larger sense its mobility. And most enhancements add to cost.

Since the Second World War the economics of tank production, governed by the complexity of manufacture and cost and the impact of a given tank design on logistics and field maintenance capabilities, have also been accepted as important in determining how many tanks a nation can afford to field in its force structure.

Some tank designs that were fielded in significant numbers, such as [Tiger I](/wiki/Tiger_I "Tiger I") and [M60A2](/wiki/M60A2_Starship "M60A2 Starship") proved to be too complex or expensive to manufacture, and made unsustainable demands on the logistics services support of the armed forces. The *affordability of the design* therefore takes precedence over the combat capability requirements.
Nowhere was this principle illustrated better than during the Second World War when two Allied designs, the Soviet [T-34](/wiki/T-34 "T-34") and the US [M4 Sherman](/wiki/M4_Sherman "M4 Sherman"), although both simple designs which accepted engineering compromises, were used successfully against more sophisticated designs by Germany that were more complex and expensive to produce, and more demanding on overstretched logistics of the Wehrmacht. Given that a tank crew will spend most of its time occupied with maintenance of the vehicle, engineering simplicity has become the primary constraint on tank design since the Second World War despite advances in mechanical, electrical and electronics technologies.

Since the Second World War, tank development has incorporated experimenting with significant mechanical changes to the tank design while focusing on technological advances in the tank's many subsystems to improve its performance. However, a number of novel designs appeared, with mixed success, including the firepower of the Soviet [IT-1](/wiki/IT-1 "IT-1") and [T-64](/wiki/T-64 "T-64"), and the crew protection of the Israeli [Merkava](/wiki/Merkava "Merkava") and Swedish [S-tank](/wiki/S-tank "S-tank"), while for decades the US's [M551](/wiki/M551 "M551") remained the only light tank deployable by parachute.

## Command, control, and communications

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Leo2A6M_li.jpg/250px-Leo2A6M_li.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leo2A6M_li.jpg)

[German Army](/wiki/German_Army "German Army") [Leopard 2](/wiki/Leopard_2 "Leopard 2")A6M incorporates [networked battlefield](/wiki/Network-centric_warfare "Network-centric warfare") technology

[Commanding and coordinating](/wiki/C4ISTAR "C4ISTAR") tanks in the field has always been subject to particular problems, particularly in the area of communications, but in modern armies these problems have been partially alleviated by [networked](/wiki/Computer_network "Computer network"), [integrated systems](/wiki/System_integration "System integration") that enable communications and contribute to enhanced [situational awareness](/wiki/Situational_awareness "Situational awareness").

### 20th century

#### World War I and Interwar period

Armoured [bulkheads](/wiki/Bulkhead_(partition) "Bulkhead (partition)"), engine noise, intervening terrain, dust and smoke, and the need to operate with hatches closed are severe detriments to communication and lead to a sense of isolation for small tank units, individual vehicles, and tank crew. Radios were not portable or robust enough to be mounted in a tank, although [Morse code](/wiki/Morse_code "Morse code") transmitters were installed in some Mark IVs at Cambrai as messaging vehicles.[[95]](#cite_note-96) The mounting of [a field telephone to the rear](/wiki/Tank_phone "Tank phone") was not a practice. During World War I when these failed or were unavailable, situation reports were sent back to headquarters by some crews releasing carrier pigeons through loopholes or hatches[[96]](#cite_note-97) and communications between vehicles was accomplished using hand signals, handheld [semaphore flags](/wiki/Flag_semaphore "Flag semaphore") which continued in use in the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army")/[Soviet Army](/wiki/Soviet_Ground_Forces "Soviet Ground Forces") through the Second and Cold wars, or by foot or horse-mounted messengers.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

#### World War II

From the beginning, the German military stressed wireless communications, equipping their combat vehicles with radios, and drilled all units to rely on disciplined radio use as a basic element of tactics. This allowed them to respond to developing threats and opportunities during battles, giving the Germans a notable tactical advantage early in the war; even where Allied tanks initially had better firepower and armour, they generally lacked individual radios.[[98]](#cite_note-99) By mid-war, Western Allied tanks adopted full use of radios, although Russian use of radios remained relatively limited.

#### Cold War era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/MerkavaMk4_ZE001m.jpg/250px-MerkavaMk4_ZE001m.jpg)](/wiki/File:MerkavaMk4_ZE001m.jpg)

[Merkava](/wiki/Merkava "Merkava") Mark 4 [main battle tank](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank") is equipped with a digital C4IS battle-management system.

On the modern battlefield an [intercom](/wiki/Intercom "Intercom") mounted in the crew helmet provides internal communications and a link to the [radio network](/wiki/Communications_network "Communications network"), and on some tanks an external intercom on the rear of the tank provides communication with co-operating infantry. Radio networks employ radio [voice procedure](/wiki/Voice_procedure "Voice procedure") to minimize confusion and "chatter".
A recent [*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] development in AFV [equipment](/wiki/Equipment_of_the_Italian_Army "Equipment of the Italian Army") and doctrine is integration of information from the [fire control system](/wiki/Fire_control_system "Fire control system"), [laser rangefinder](/wiki/Laser_rangefinder "Laser rangefinder"), [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") and terrain information via [hardened](/wiki/Electromagnetic_pulse "Electromagnetic pulse") military specification [electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics") and a [battlefield network](/wiki/Computer_networks "Computer networks") to display information on enemy targets and friendly units on a [monitor](/wiki/Computer_monitor "Computer monitor") in the tank. The sensor data can be sourced from nearby tanks, planes, [UAVs](/wiki/Unmanned_aerial_vehicles "Unmanned aerial vehicles") or, in the future infantry (such as the US [Future Force Warrior](/wiki/Future_Force_Warrior "Future Force Warrior") project). This improves the tank commander's [situational awareness](/wiki/Situational_awareness "Situational awareness") and ability to [navigate](/wiki/Navigate "Navigate") the battlefield and select and engage targets. In addition to easing the reporting burden by automatically logging all orders and actions, orders are sent via the network with text and graphical overlays. This is known as [Network-centric warfare](/wiki/Network-centric_warfare "Network-centric warfare") by the US, [Network Enabled Capability](/wiki/Network_Enabled_Capability "Network Enabled Capability") (UK) or [Digital Army Battle Management System](/w/index.php?title=Digital_Army_(IDF)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Digital Army (IDF) (page does not exist)") צי"ד (Israel). Advanced battle tanks, including the [K-2 Black Panther](/wiki/K2_Black_Panther "K2 Black Panther"), have taken up the first major step forward in adopting a fully [radar integrated Fire Control System](/w/index.php?title=Radar_integrated_Fire_Control_System&action=edit&redlink=1 "Radar integrated Fire Control System (page does not exist)") which allows it to detect tanks from a further distance and identify it as a friend-or-foe as well as increasing the tank's accuracy as well as its capability to lock onto tanks.

#### 21st century

See also: [Military communications](/wiki/Military_communications "Military communications") and [C4ISTAR](/wiki/C4ISTAR "C4ISTAR")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Limpid_Armor_LCG_DSS.jpg/250px-Limpid_Armor_LCG_DSS.jpg)](/wiki/File:Limpid_Armor_LCG_DSS.jpg)

Circular review system of the company LimpidArmor

Performing situational awareness and communication is the one of four primary MBT functions in the 21st century.[[99]](#cite_note-MBT-100)
To improve the crew's situational awareness MBTs use [circular review system](/wiki/Circular_review_system "Circular review system") with a combination of [Augmented reality](/wiki/Augmented_reality "Augmented reality") and [Artificial Intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_Intelligence "Artificial Intelligence") technologies.[[100]](#cite_note-AI-101)

Further advancements in tank defense systems have led to the development of [active protection systems](/wiki/Active_protection_system "Active protection system"), which may be classified as either:

* **Soft-kill** – Soft-kill protection systems use integrated on-board [radar warning receivers](/wiki/Radar_warning_receiver "Radar warning receiver") which can detect incoming anti-tank missiles and projectiles. Once detected, measures such as [smoke screens](/wiki/Smoke_screen "Smoke screen") or [smoke grenades](/wiki/Smoke_grenade "Smoke grenade") are deployed, interfering with the incoming missile's tracking system, causing it to miss the tank or to deactivate.
* **Hard-kill** – The more advanced approach involves destroying the incoming enemy missile or projectile by deploying anti-missile projectiles. This is seen more reliable protection.

Both these active protection systems can be found on several [main battle tanks](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank") including the [K2 Black Panther](/wiki/K2_Black_Panther "K2 Black Panther"), the Merkava and the Leopard 2A7.

However, later in the War, a number of Mk IV Tanks were fitted with grapnels to remove barbed wire. They were designated "Wire Cutters" and had the large letters "W.C." painted on their rear armour.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
